articles.title: Dashboard and Map
articles.headline: Dashboard and Map
articles.description: How the AtlasDays dashboard works: stats, trackers, period filters, interactive map, day counting, and share cards.
articles.intro: How to read the dashboard, change periods, understand the map, and use the share features.

section_contexts.heading: What the dashboard is for
section_contexts.summary: The dashboard turns your trip history into a period-based summary. It is the place to answer questions like: how many countries have I visited this year, how many days have I spent abroad, and what does that look like on the map?

section_contexts.heading: The main dashboard stats
section_contexts.summary: The four stat tiles at the top are tappable — each opens a detail sheet with the full breakdown.
detail_points: Countries: how many distinct countries you visited in the period. The percentage denominator depends on your counting mode in Settings: UN 195 (standard member states), UN 195+2 (adds Kosovo and Taiwan), or All Places (includes territories and dependencies like Hong Kong, Guam, and Aruba). Tap to see the full country list with days per country.
Trips: how many trips fall inside the selected period. Tap to see the list.

Days Abroad: total days spent outside your home country. If you chose no fixed residence, this counts all days with a recorded trip. Tap to see the breakdown between days in tracker-relevant countries and days elsewhere.
Continents: how many continents your trips span. Tap to see countries grouped by continent.

section_contexts.heading: Trackers on the dashboard
section_contexts.summary: If you have trackers set up, they appear as cards below the stats. Each card shows the tracker name, current day count, threshold, and a progress bar. The bar shifts from your accent color to orange past two-thirds, and to red past five-sixths — so you can tell at a glance how close you are to a limit or a goal.
detail_points: For upper-limit trackers, the card shows "X days remaining" or "Over by Y days". For target trackers, it shows "X days needed" or "Target reached". You can reorder tracker cards by dragging, and collapse or expand the whole tracker section.

section_contexts.heading: Changing the period
section_contexts.summary: The dashboard has two period modes. Year mode lets you pick a specific year or All Time. Custom range mode lets you choose a window like the last 7, 30, 90, 180, or 365 days, or set any start and end date manually. Switch between them using the control at the top of the dashboard.
detail_points: This matters because the same trip history can answer different questions depending on the time window. A Schengen tracker cares about the last 180 days. A tax question usually cares about a calendar year.

section_contexts.heading: Using the map
section_contexts.summary: The dashboard shows a mini world map. Tapping it opens the full-screen interactive map where you can tap any country to see its trip details.
detail_points: Visited countries are highlighted in your chosen accent color. Countries you only transited through (airport layovers marked as transit) appear in a separate color so you can tell the difference. Very small countries like Vatican City, Singapore, and Malta show up as dot markers since their territory is too small to see otherwise.
The map reflects the currently selected period, so switching from "2024" to "All Time" changes which countries are highlighted.

section_contexts.heading: How day counting works
section_contexts.summary: A detail worth knowing: AtlasDays counts both the arrival day and the departure day. A trip from January 1 to January 3 is 3 days, not 2. This matches how most immigration authorities count presence.
detail_points: If you have overlapping trips to the same country, shared days are only counted once. Two trips that both cover January 5 do not produce 2 days for that date — they produce 1. Ongoing trips (no end date) use today as the effective end for counting purposes.

section_contexts.heading: Share cards
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays can generate shareable cards from your dashboard data, including a map snapshot and your key stats. You can swipe through a carousel of card styles and toggle each card between light and dark appearance independently from your system theme. Save to Photos or share directly.
detail_points: The cards are rendered from your actual trip data for the selected period, not composed manually.
Trackers and limits explains what the dashboard is summarizing. Trip modes and import explains how to improve the underlying data.

articles.title: Getting Started with AtlasDays
articles.headline: Getting Started with AtlasDays
articles.description: How to start using AtlasDays: timeline, dashboard, trackers, first trips, and how to choose between Exact Dates and Year.
articles.intro: What AtlasDays is for, what the three tabs do, and how to build a useful record without overthinking the setup.

section_contexts.heading: What AtlasDays is designed to solve
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays is for people who need a reliable country-by-country travel record. That usually means one or more of these things matter to you: visa day limits, residence or tax presence thresholds, border questions, or reconstructing travel history later for paperwork.
detail_points: The app is built around one idea: keep the trip record itself clean first . Once your trip history is solid, the day counts, map, dashboard, and trackers become useful automatically.

section_contexts.heading: The three tabs
section_contexts.summary: This is where you add and manage trips. Think of it as your source of truth. Trips are grouped by year with collapsible sections, and you can search across country names, notes, and years. Swipe left on any trip to delete it — you get a few seconds to undo.
detail_points: This is where you review totals, trackers, countries visited, days abroad, continents, and the map for a selected period. You can switch between year-based and custom date-range views. Each stat tile is tappable — it opens a detail sheet with the full breakdown.
This is where you choose your home country, appearance and accent color, iCloud sync, CSV import/export, and privacy-related options like auto-detect and notifications.

section_contexts.heading: Your best first setup
section_contexts.summary: Add your current or most recent trips first. Start with recent history you already know is accurate.
detail_points: Set your home country in Settings. This determines what counts as "abroad" in the dashboard stats. If you do not have one fixed base, choose the no-fixed-residence option — days abroad will then count all days with a recorded trip.
Add a tracker only after your trip history has enough detail to make the count useful.
Use approximate or unknown-date trips for older history instead of inventing fake exact dates.

section_contexts.heading: Choose the right trip mode first
section_contexts.summary: If a trip affects a real count, enter it as Exact Dates . Use Year when your memory is rough and you want to preserve the trip honestly without making up precise days. If you cannot even place the trip in a year yet, choose Unknown in the year picker. Exact-date trips can be ongoing or upcoming. Year entries cannot be future-dated.
detail_points: That distinction matters later: exact trips feed precise day totals and tracker math, while approximate trips preserve history and context.
Good rule of thumb: if you are not sure of an old trip’s exact dates, record it approximately rather than leaving it out entirely. AtlasDays is designed for incomplete real-world history.

section_contexts.heading: When to add a tracker
section_contexts.summary: Trackers are useful when you care about a specific threshold or goal. If you just want a clean travel record first, build the record and add trackers once the trip data is reasonably complete.

section_contexts.heading: What to do if your history is messy
section_contexts.summary: Use the CSV import and export tools in Settings if you already have a spreadsheet or rough travel notes. The app also includes a built-in prompt you can copy into ChatGPT or Claude along with your raw data to generate import-ready CSV. The import step shows a preview with per-row validation before anything is saved, and it automatically skips duplicates of trips you already have.
detail_points: Learn about trackers and limits , or jump to Trip Modes and Import if your main job is rebuilding older travel data.

articles.title: Trip Modes and Import
articles.headline: Trip Modes and Import
articles.description: How AtlasDays handles Exact Dates vs Year trips, unknown entries, transit, and CSV import/export.
articles.intro: How Exact Dates and Year differ, how Unknown fits in, and how CSV import matches those same formats.
articles.plan_notes: Make the contrast between exact dates and year-only or unknown trips visually obvious without relying on readable UI text.

section_contexts.heading: Why AtlasDays has two main trip modes
section_contexts.summary: The app uses two primary entry modes because real travel history usually comes in two qualities: trips you know exactly, and trips you can only place by year or not confidently at all. AtlasDays lets you store both without forcing fake precision.

section_contexts.heading: Exact Dates
section_contexts.summary: Use Exact Dates when you know the actual arrival and departure dates, or when you want to pre-add a future trip. This is the only mode that supports ongoing trips and upcoming trips.
detail_points: Exact-date trips are also what power precise tracker math, dashboard day totals, and the most reliable country-day calculations. If the number matters for a visa, tax, or border question, this is the mode you want whenever possible.

section_contexts.heading: Year
section_contexts.summary: Use Year when you know which year a trip belongs in but not the exact arrival and departure dates. You can save a single year or a multi-year range when a trip clearly spans more than one calendar year.
detail_points: This mode is designed for reconstructed history. It keeps the record honest without pretending you know the exact entry and exit days.
Year entries cannot be saved in the future.
The start and end values are years only. There is no separate month picker anymore.
Year entries help with history and context, but they do not generate exact day counts.

section_contexts.heading: Unknown
section_contexts.summary: Inside Year , the year picker includes an Unknown option. Choosing it creates a country-only memory with no stored date fields. It is useful when you know you visited a place but cannot place it confidently in time yet.
detail_points: Unknown-date trips appear at the bottom of the timeline in the No Date section. They preserve the fact of the visit, but they do not drive exact-day calculations.
Best practice: if a trip affects a real limit, keep it exact. Use Year or Unknown entries to preserve incomplete history without contaminating the precise day math.

section_contexts.heading: How the mode changes what you see
section_contexts.summary: Ongoing and Upcoming sections: only exact-date trips can appear there.
detail_points: Year sections: year entries are grouped into a year section instead of Ongoing or Upcoming.
No Date section: unknown-date trips go there.

Dashboard custom ranges: year entries only appear if your selected range fully contains at least one year they could fall into. AtlasDays avoids pretending a precise day count when the real date could have fallen outside the range.

section_contexts.heading: Transit
section_contexts.summary: Both Exact Dates and Year entries can be marked as Transit from the extra options area. Transit entries stay in your history but are excluded from day counts.

section_contexts.heading: CSV import and export
section_contexts.summary: The CSV format mirrors the same date models the app stores:
detail_points: AtlasDays does not let you mix exact dates with year-only values in the same imported row. One row must use one precision model.
Exact dates: fill Start Date and End Date.
Ongoing exact trip: fill Start Date and leave End Date empty.
Year: put a 4-digit year in Start Date. Add End Date only if the trip spans multiple years.
Unknown date: leave both Start Date and End Date empty.

section_contexts.heading: Import details
section_contexts.summary: The CSV importer accepts both full country names and ISO country codes, and auto-detects whether your file uses commas, semicolons, or tabs as the delimiter. The current export format is five columns: Country , Start Date , End Date , Airside , and Notes . Use yyyy-mm-dd for exact trips, a bare 4-digit year for Year entries, or leave both date fields empty for Unknown.
detail_points: Older 9-column CSV files with month and year columns still import for backward compatibility, but AtlasDays now ignores the legacy month columns. The app shows a preview with per-row validation — unknown countries, invalid dates, and format problems are flagged before you confirm.

If you import trips that already exist in your history, duplicates are automatically skipped. You can also download a sample CSV template from the import screen that shows examples of all date precisions.

section_contexts.heading: Overlap detection
section_contexts.summary: If two exact-date trips share nights in the same or different countries, AtlasDays flags the overlap with a banner on the timeline. You can resolve overlaps by splitting one trip, trimming the end or start, merging both into one trip, or deciding later. This only applies to exact-date, non-transit trips.

section_contexts.heading: The AI prompt helper
section_contexts.summary: The app includes a built-in prompt you can copy into ChatGPT, Claude, or another assistant together with your raw travel notes. Its job is to convert messy source material into AtlasDays CSV faster, not to replace your review.
detail_points: Getting started explains how these modes fit into normal use. Trackers and limits explains why exact dates matter when a counter needs to be actionable.

This image supports product documentation for AtlasDays, a private iPhone app for trip history, visa days, and residency tracking. It should feel product-adjacent without recreating a literal app screenshot, and should still carry some travel-product personality.
Use a premium travel-tech editorial illustration style that fits AtlasDays: dark charcoal and cool slate as the base, deep oceanic navy depth, cyan-blue route accents, pale ice-blue surfaces, and small restrained warm highlights for contrast. The image should feel premium, modern, mobile-first, and travel-oriented: calm but not sterile, precise but not academic, aspirational without becoming generic travel-advertising fluff. Favor layered depth, subtle glow, motion, route energy, and destination atmosphere over flat icon-pack graphics. Do not make it whimsical, photoreal stock-photo-like, or cartoonish.
Topic concept: trip records moving from messy inputs into a clean, structured timeline.
Supporting context: How AtlasDays handles Exact Dates vs Year trips, unknown entries, transit, and CSV import/export.
This artwork will appear beneath an existing webpage title and subtitle, so the image itself must not contain any heading, subtitle, sentence, or label. Minimal threshold numerals are acceptable, but avoid poster-style copy or UI-like text.
Do not make the image look like a poster, slide, infographic header, or book cover.
Use a dark neutral background that separates cleanly from both the light and dark website themes. Blue should appear as an accent, not as a full flat background.
Visual focus: trip records moving from messy inputs into a clean, structured timeline.
Helpful motifs: calendar fragments, CSV-like rows, timeline cards, route dots.
The image should feel like it belongs to a product people are excited to use for travel, not just a neutral reference article.
Bring in some sense of journey, movement, place, or destination atmosphere while staying clean and legible on the page.
When the image uses paper, cards, tables, or document-like surfaces, tint them with pale ice-blue, mist blue, or cool blue-gray rather than stark pure white so they integrate better with light mode.
Avoid large flat white blocks. Keep highlights soft, slightly cool, and premium.
Keep the image subtly connected to AtlasDays by using travel-tracking-adjacent motifs such as route lines, day-count chips, tidy cards, world highlights, or private-device context, but do not show a literal app screenshot.
Avoid readable text, letters, words, labels, titles, map labels, typographic elements, UI screenshots, government seals, watermarks, stock-photo cliches, passport-stamp collages, fake official forms, brand logos, or cluttered layouts. Avoid stiff textbook-diagram energy, generic corporate SaaS art, or dry flat iconography. If a counting concept is needed, represent it abstractly with bars, chips, rings, windows, or markers. Minimal numerals are acceptable only when they function as an abstract threshold symbol rather than readable UI text or copy.
Intro context: "How Exact Dates and Year differ, how Unknown fits in, and how CSV import matches those same formats."
Additional global art direction: Keep the images calm, premium, and clear on a documentation-heavy website. Favor strong silhouettes, clean geometry, and a travel-compliance tone over busy scenes or stock-photo energy.
Additional article-specific notes: Make the contrast between exact dates and year-only or unknown trips visually obvious without relying on readable UI text.
Slot role: top-of-article hero image.
Composition goal: Create the main article cover image with one dominant subject and two or three supporting elements. It should feel like a proper top-of-page hero image that can sit under the page title.
Explicit scene brief: Show AtlasDays turning messy travel memory into a clear system: one side has softer year-only or uncertain trip blocks, the other has crisp exact-date trip cards, and both feed into one elegant timeline. It should feel like a premium travel app solving a real problem, not a filing cabinet.
Preferred visual kind: trip-mode overview
Differentiate clearly from these sibling slots: body-1: precision-vs-approximation comparison
Must include these visual cues: both approximate and exact trip shapes, a single timeline or record system connecting them, subtle travel movement or route context
Extra slot-specific avoidances: plain spreadsheet-only composition, bureaucratic or office paperwork feel

articles.title: Privacy, Location, and Sync
articles.headline: Privacy, Location, and Sync
articles.description: How AtlasDays handles on-device data, optional iCloud sync, country detection, and what the app does not store.
articles.intro: How AtlasDays handles on-device data, optional iCloud sync, country-detection suggestions, and what the app does not store.
articles.plan_notes: Focus on local-device privacy and optional sync. Do not use surveillance or creepy location-tracking imagery.

section_contexts.heading: No account required
section_contexts.summary: The AtlasDays app is designed so you can use it without creating an account or signing in to an AtlasDays service.

section_contexts.heading: On-device data by default
section_contexts.summary: Your trip history and tracker calculations live on your device. The app is designed around a local record rather than a cloud-first account system.

section_contexts.heading: Optional iCloud sync
section_contexts.summary: If you enable iCloud sync in Settings, your trips and trackers sync across your devices through your personal Apple iCloud account. This uses Apple's CloudKit — your data goes to your iCloud storage, not to an AtlasDays server.
detail_points: Separately, your preferences (appearance, accent color, home country, and other settings) sync through iCloud key-value storage automatically if you are signed into iCloud. This works even if you have not enabled the full data sync toggle, so your settings carry over to a new device or after a reinstall without extra setup.
If you reinstall the app and iCloud sync was previously enabled, your trip data will recover from CloudKit once you turn the sync toggle back on.

section_contexts.heading: How country detection works
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays can optionally detect that you have entered a new country and suggest a trip. It uses iOS significant location changes — the same low-power system other travel apps use — not continuous GPS tracking. The app only wakes up when iOS determines you have moved a significant distance, then checks which country you are in. This has minimal battery impact.
detail_points: When a new country is detected, the app suggests a trip either as an in-app banner (if the app is open) or as a notification (if it is in the background). You choose whether to accept, edit, or dismiss the suggestion. The app only suggests once per country crossing — it will not re-suggest the same country until you leave and re-enter.
You can leave auto-detect off and log trips manually.
AtlasDays does not store a location history or send it to an AtlasDays server.
You can revoke location access at any time in iOS Settings.

section_contexts.heading: Notifications
section_contexts.summary: Notifications serve two purposes: trip suggestions when you enter a new country, and tracker milestone alerts (like "7 days remaining on your Schengen limit"). Both are local notifications scheduled on your device — nothing goes through a server. You can turn off trip suggestions and tracker alerts independently in Settings.
detail_points: Read the full app privacy page , or go to trip modes and import if your main question is how AtlasDays stores travel records.

articles.title: Trackers and Limits
articles.headline: Trackers and Limits
articles.description: How AtlasDays trackers really work: presets, rolling vs per-stay vs yearly windows, upper limits vs targets, and when exact dates matter.
articles.intro: How to choose the right tracker, what each window type actually counts, and where exact dates still matter.
articles.plan_notes: The image should make rolling windows, yearly thresholds, and per-stay counting feel structured and understandable.

section_contexts.heading: What a tracker actually does
section_contexts.summary: Your trip history is the raw record. A tracker is a counting rule applied to that record. The same trip can affect multiple trackers at once, because each tracker asks a different question about the same travel history.
detail_points: That also means tracker reliability depends on the quality of the underlying trip data. Exact-date trips drive the precise day math. Approximate, year-only, and unknown-date trips keep the record complete, but they do not generate exact tracker day totals.

section_contexts.heading: Choose the right preset
section_contexts.summary: This starts as a yearly target with a default threshold of 183 days. Use it when you want to prove or reach presence in one country for tax or residency reasons. After creation, you can still point it at a specific year or a custom fiscal-year period.
detail_points: This is a preconfigured upper-limit rolling tracker : 90 days across the full Schengen set in the last 180 days. Use it when you want one combined Schengen counter instead of separate country trackers.

This is a per-stay upper-limit tracker . Use it when each continuous stay matters more than your total across the year. AtlasDays anchors this kind of tracker to a tracking-from date, because per-stay counting needs a clear start point.

section_contexts.heading: Rolling vs Per Stay vs Yearly
section_contexts.summary: A rolling tracker counts days inside the last N days. The window moves forward every day, so old days fall off automatically. This is the right shape for rules like Schengen 90/180.
detail_points: A per-stay tracker is about one continuous stay, not the total across many trips. If you have an ongoing exact-date trip in that country set, AtlasDays follows that current stay. If you do not, the tracker falls back to the most recent completed stay after the tracking-from date.
A yearly tracker counts inside a Jan 1 to Dec 31 period by default. You can also switch it to a specific year or a custom fiscal year if your real-world rule does not follow the calendar year.

section_contexts.heading: Stay under vs reach target
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays supports two tracker directions. Stay under is for limits, where you want a warning as you approach a maximum. Reach target is for goals, where you want to accumulate at least a certain number of days.
detail_points: Important: tracker calculations use only trips with exact dates. If a rule is legally or financially important, upgrade the relevant trips to exact dates before you rely on the number.

section_contexts.heading: What counts and what does not
section_contexts.summary: Exact-date trips: these drive tracker day totals. Both the arrival day and the departure day count — a trip from the 1st to the 3rd is 3 days. If two trips overlap in the same country, shared days are counted once, not twice.
detail_points: Ongoing trips: a trip with no end date uses today as the effective end, so the count grows each day.
Approximate, year-only, and unknown trips: these preserve history and context, but they do not feed exact day math.
Transit trips: if a trip is marked as transit, AtlasDays counts 0 days for it. The country still appears on the map in a separate color.

section_contexts.heading: Tracker alerts
section_contexts.summary: Each tracker can send local notifications as you approach a limit or reach a goal. For upper-limit trackers, you get alerts at 7 days remaining, 1 day remaining, and when the limit is reached. For target trackers, you get an alert when the target is met. These are scheduled automatically based on your current trip data and update whenever your trips change.

section_contexts.heading: Common setup patterns
section_contexts.summary: Read how trip modes affect tracker accuracy , see how trackers appear in the dashboard , or visit Learn if you want a broader Schengen explainer.
detail_points: I need a Schengen counter: use the Schengen preset and keep the relevant trips exact-dated.
I need to prove 183 days in one country this year: use Residence, choose the country, and keep the relevant year exact.
I need to know how long my current stay has been: use Visa Limit with a per-stay window and a clear tracking-from date.
I want to spend at least 30 days somewhere this year: use Travel Goal.

articles.title: 183-Day Tax Residency Rule: What Travelers Need to Know
articles.headline: 183-Day Tax Residency Rule: What Travelers Need to Know
articles.description: How the 183-day tax residency rule works, which countries use it, and how to track your days to avoid triggering unexpected tax obligations.
articles.intro: How the 183-day rule works, which countries use it, and how to track your days to avoid triggering unexpected tax obligations.

section_contexts.heading: What is the 183-day rule?
section_contexts.summary: The 183-day rule is one of the most widely used tests for determining tax residency . In simple terms, if you spend 183 days or more in a country during a given period, that country may consider you a tax resident — meaning you could be required to pay taxes there on your income, regardless of where it was earned.
detail_points: The rule exists in the domestic tax laws of many countries and is also embedded in most double taxation treaties . It serves as a straightforward, objective measure: if you are physically present in a country for at least 183 days, you likely have strong enough ties to be taxed there.

section_contexts.heading: Why 183 days specifically?
section_contexts.summary: The number 183 is not arbitrary. A standard year has 365 days, and half of that (rounded up) is 183. The underlying logic is simple: if you spend more than half the year in a country, you have a sufficient connection to that country to be subject to its tax system . This threshold provides a clear, measurable line that both taxpayers and tax authorities can apply without ambiguity.
detail_points: Many bilateral tax treaties between countries also use 183 days as the tiebreaker for determining which country has the primary right to tax an individual's income, particularly employment income earned while temporarily working abroad.

section_contexts.heading: Countries that use the 183-day rule
section_contexts.summary: The 183-day threshold is used by a large number of countries around the world, though the details vary significantly:
detail_points: Important variation: Not all countries count the same way. Some use the calendar year (January to December), others use a fiscal year that starts in April, July, or October. Some count any partial day as a full day of presence, while others require you to be present at midnight. Always check the specific rules for the country in question.

United States: Uses a "substantial presence test" that involves a weighted formula across three years, but 183 days of physical presence in a single year automatically qualifies you as a tax resident.
United Kingdom: Has a more complex Statutory Residence Test, but spending 183 days or more in the UK in a tax year (which runs April 6 to April 5) makes you automatically resident.
Most EU member states: Countries like Germany, France, Spain, Italy, and the Netherlands all use 183 days as a key residency threshold, though each defines the counting period differently.
Australia: Uses 183 days as one of several tests, with the tax year running from July 1 to June 30.

section_contexts.heading: Common pitfalls
section_contexts.summary: The 183-day rule sounds straightforward, but travelers frequently run into complications:
detail_points: Partial days counting as full days: In many jurisdictions, even arriving late at night or departing early in the morning counts as a full day of presence. Transit days may also count.

Different tax year start dates: The UK tax year starts on April 6, Australia's on July 1, and the US uses the calendar year. If you split time between countries, you may be counting against different windows simultaneously.

Tax residency in multiple countries: It is entirely possible to be considered a tax resident in two or more countries at the same time. Spending 183 days in one country does not automatically free you from obligations in another.

Double taxation: Without proper planning or the protection of a tax treaty, you could owe taxes on the same income to two different countries. Tax treaties help resolve this, but only if you are aware of them and claim the appropriate relief.

Other residency triggers: Many countries can deem you a tax resident even below 183 days if you have a permanent home, family ties, economic interests, or habitual abode there. The 183-day test is often just one of several criteria.

section_contexts.heading: How to track your days
section_contexts.summary: Accurate day-counting is essential if you move between countries regularly. Many digital nomads, remote workers, and frequent travelers rely on spreadsheets or manual notes, but this approach is fragile. A single missed entry or miscounted day can mean the difference between being a tax resident and not.
detail_points: The challenge gets harder when you visit multiple countries in a single year. You need to track your days per country, across different counting periods, and understand how partial days are treated — all at the same time.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute tax or legal advice. Tax residency rules are complex and vary by jurisdiction. AtlasDays helps you count your days of presence in each country — it does not interpret tax law or provide tax guidance. Always consult a qualified tax professional for advice about your specific situation.
Schengen 90/180-Day Rule Explained
How to Track Your Travel Days (And Why Spreadsheets Fail)
Digital Nomad Visa Day Limits: A Country-by-Country Overview

articles.title: Country Counting Rules: Every Major List Compared
articles.headline: Country Counting Rules: Every Major List Compared
articles.description: UN, ISO, FIFA, Olympics, TCC, NomadMania — every system for counting countries visited works differently. Here's what each one includes, how many entries it has, and which list you should use.
articles.intro: UN, ISO, FIFA, Olympics, TCC, NomadMania — every system counts differently. Here's what each one includes and why it matters.

section_contexts.heading: Why there's no single answer
section_contexts.summary: "How many countries have you visited?" sounds like a simple question. It isn't. The answer depends entirely on which list you use — and there are at least half a dozen widely used ones, each built for a different purpose.
detail_points: A trip to Hong Kong counts as one additional entry on the Travelers' Century Club list, but zero extra countries on the UN list (it's part of China). Visit Kosovo and you can add it to your FIFA count, but not your UN tally. Fly to Guam and you've been to a new ISO code, a separate Olympic territory, and a TCC entry — but you never left the United States.
None of these systems are wrong. They just measure different things. Here's how each one works.

section_contexts.heading: United Nations (195)
section_contexts.summary: The most commonly cited number. The UN recognizes 193 member states plus 2 non-member observer states : Vatican City and Palestine. That gives you 195.
detail_points: This is a political list, not a travel list. Membership requires approval by the General Assembly, which means geopolitics determines who's on it. Kosovo, Taiwan, and Western Sahara are not included despite being distinct travel destinations with their own borders, currencies, and entry requirements.

When someone says "I've been to 50 countries," they usually mean sovereign states in the UN sense — or something close to it. It's the default assumption in casual conversation, and the most conservative way to count.

section_contexts.heading: ISO 3166 (249 codes)
section_contexts.summary: ISO 3166 is the international standard for country codes — the two-letter abbreviations used by airlines, shipping companies, databases, and most software. It includes 249 entries: sovereign states, territories, dependencies, and special areas.
detail_points: This is the list that gives Hong Kong its own code (HK), Guam its own code (GU), and Aruba its own code (AW). It's more comprehensive than the UN list because it cares about practical identification, not political sovereignty.

The tradeoff is that some entries aren't really "places" in the travel sense. Antarctica has a code (AQ). So does the United States Minor Outlying Islands (UM), a grouping of scattered, mostly uninhabited atolls. ISO 3166 is useful as infrastructure — AtlasDays uses it internally — but it's not designed as a travel checklist.

section_contexts.heading: FIFA (211 members)
section_contexts.summary: FIFA recognizes 211 member associations — more than the UN, because sports federations operate independently from political recognition. If a territory can field a football team and its regional confederation accepts it, FIFA can grant membership regardless of what the UN thinks.
detail_points: That's why Kosovo, Taiwan (competing as Chinese Taipei), Palestine, Gibraltar, and several Caribbean and Pacific island territories all have FIFA membership. The British Virgin Islands, Guam, Puerto Rico, and American Samoa each have their own football associations.

Some travelers — particularly football fans who follow their national team to away matches — use the FIFA list as their benchmark. It strikes a middle ground: more inclusive than the UN, but not as sprawling as the TCC list.

section_contexts.heading: Olympics (206 NOCs)
section_contexts.summary: The International Olympic Committee recognizes 206 National Olympic Committees — slightly fewer than FIFA. Like FIFA, the IOC doesn't require UN membership. Puerto Rico, Guam, the US Virgin Islands, American Samoa, Hong Kong, and Palestine all compete independently at the Olympics.
detail_points: The difference from FIFA comes down to administrative decisions. Some territories that have FIFA membership don't have a recognized NOC, and vice versa. The lists are close but not identical.

The Olympic list isn't commonly used for country counting, but it's worth knowing about because it illustrates an important point: there is no single authority that decides what counts as a "country." Even international organizations can't agree.

section_contexts.heading: Travelers' Century Club (~330)
section_contexts.summary: The Travelers' Century Club (TCC) is the oldest travel counting organization, founded in 1954 for people who've visited 100 or more "countries and territories." Their list currently includes roughly 330 entries and is updated periodically.
detail_points: The TCC list is generous by design. It includes island groups, overseas territories, exclaves, enclaves, and geographical subregions that other lists would lump together. European Turkey and Asian Turkey are counted as separate entries. Alaska and Hawaii are separated from the contiguous United States. Easter Island is distinct from mainland Chile. Peninsular Malaysia and East Malaysia (Borneo) are split.

This is the list most commonly used by serious country collectors — people whose goal is to visit as much of the world as possible. The higher number creates a longer runway and rewards travelers for reaching remote or unusual territories.

section_contexts.heading: NomadMania (1,301 regions)
section_contexts.summary: NomadMania takes granularity to its logical extreme. Their system divides the world into over 1,300 distinct regions , making it by far the most detailed travel tracking framework available.
detail_points: Visiting "France" isn't one checkmark — it's Ile-de-France, Provence-Alpes-Cote d'Azur, Corsica, French Guiana, Reunion, Martinique, and more, each counted separately. The United States is broken into individual states. India is split into its states and union territories. Even small countries may be divided into two or three regions.

NomadMania is designed for hardcore travelers who've already been to 100+ countries and want more resolution. When the UN list or TCC list starts to feel "done," NomadMania provides a new challenge with years of travel still ahead. It's the endgame list — the one that's practically impossible to complete.

section_contexts.heading: Which list should you use?
section_contexts.summary: There's no universally correct answer. The right list depends on what you're tracking and why.
detail_points: The main rule is consistency. Pick one system and stick with it. Mixing lists — counting Hong Kong separately when it suits you but not when it doesn't — makes your number meaningless. Be clear about which list you're using, especially when comparing counts with other travelers.
For visa and legal purposes: use sovereign states. The UN list (or a close equivalent) is what immigration authorities care about.

For casual travel tracking: the UN 195 is the simplest starting point. Some people prefer 197, adding Kosovo and Taiwan as widely recognized travel destinations that happen to lack full UN membership.
For serious country collecting: the TCC list or a territory-inclusive list gives you a more meaningful total that reflects the diversity of places you've actually been.
For maximum granularity: NomadMania's 1,301 regions reward depth over breadth and keep the challenge alive long after you've crossed the 100-country mark.

section_contexts.heading: What AtlasDays offers
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays gives you three counting modes so you don't have to choose a single system forever:
detail_points: Your map shows everything you've visited regardless of which mode is active. The counting mode only affects the denominator — the "out of Y countries" part of your stat. Switch between modes anytime to see your progress through different lenses.
AtlasDays supports 195, 197, or every territory — your map, your rules.
UN 195 — the standard 193 member states plus Vatican City and Palestine.
UN 195+2 — adds Kosovo and Taiwan for travelers who consider them distinct destinations.
All Places — includes territories, dependencies, and special regions for a complete picture.

articles.title: Digital Nomad Visa Day Limits: A Country-by-Country Overview
articles.headline: Digital Nomad Visa Day Limits: A Country-by-Country Overview
articles.description: Day limits and stay rules for popular digital nomad destinations — from Schengen to Thailand, Portugal to Colombia.
articles.intro: Day limits and stay rules for popular digital nomad destinations — from Schengen to Thailand, Portugal to Colombia.

section_contexts.heading: Why day limits matter for nomads
section_contexts.summary: Tourists visit one country and go home. Digital nomads hop between countries, often visiting several in a single year. That means you're not just tracking one visa clock — you're juggling multiple overlapping stay limits simultaneously. Lose track of one, and you risk overstaying, facing fines, deportation, or future entry bans.
detail_points: The challenge is that every country has its own rules, and those rules don't coordinate with each other. A week in Portugal counts toward your Schengen days, but a week in Thailand runs on an entirely separate clock. Understanding each destination's limits before you book your flight is the first step to staying legal on the road.

section_contexts.heading: Popular destinations and their day limits
section_contexts.summary: Visa-free for many nationalities. The 90/180 rule applies across all 29 Schengen member states combined — days in France, Germany, and Spain all count toward the same limit. The window rolls forward daily, making it tricky to calculate manually.
detail_points: Many passport holders receive 60 days on arrival. This can be extended by 30 days at a local immigration office for a fee, giving you up to 90 days per entry. Longer stays require a tourist visa or specific visa category.

As a Schengen member, Portugal's visa-free stays count toward your 90/180 Schengen days. However, Portugal also offers a dedicated digital nomad visa valid for one year and renewable, designed for remote workers with income from outside Portugal.

section_contexts.heading: The hidden complexity
section_contexts.summary: Day limits are just the surface. Underneath, several less obvious rules can trip up even experienced nomads:
detail_points: Tax residency triggers: many countries consider you a tax resident after a certain number of days (often 183 per year). You might be legally within your visa limit but accidentally triggering tax obligations.
Overlapping windows: the Schengen rolling window, Thailand's per-entry count, and Colombia's calendar-year cap all operate on different timelines. They don't pause while you're in another country.
Re-entry rules: some countries require a minimum time outside the country before you can re-enter or start a new visa-free stay. A quick "visa run" to a neighboring country may not reset your clock.

section_contexts.heading: Why tracking matters more for nomads than tourists
section_contexts.summary: A tourist takes one trip and comes home. A digital nomad might visit five or six countries in a year, each with its own visa clock ticking independently. Add in tax residency thresholds across multiple jurisdictions, and the margin for error shrinks fast.
detail_points: Spreadsheets work until they don't — one missed entry, one miscounted day, and you're at risk. The more countries you visit, the more critical it becomes to have a reliable system that tracks every window automatically.

section_contexts.heading: Set this up in AtlasDays
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays handles exactly the kind of multi-country tracking nomads need. The built-in Schengen tracker handles the rolling 90/180-day window automatically. For every other destination, you can create a custom tracker with the matching rule type — rolling window, calendar year, or per-stay — and the app counts your days in real time. All your trackers live side by side, so you can see every active visa clock at a glance.
detail_points: Note: Visa rules and day limits change frequently. Always verify current requirements with official government sources or your country's embassy before traveling. The information above is provided as a general overview and may not reflect the latest regulations.
AtlasDays keeps your visa windows and residency counters in one place.
Schengen 90/180-Day Rule Explained
183-Day Tax Residency Rule: What Travelers Need to Know
How to Track Your Travel Days (And Why Spreadsheets Fail)

articles.title: Does a Layover Count as Visiting a Country?
articles.headline: Does a Layover Count as Visiting a Country?
articles.description: Does a layover count as visiting a country? For immigration it's clear-cut. For your travel list, it's personal. Here's how to think about it.
articles.intro: For immigration it's clear. For your travel list, it's a personal call. Here's how to think about it.

section_contexts.heading: Two different questions
section_contexts.summary: This seems like a single question, but it's actually two completely separate ones. The first: does immigration count a layover as visiting? The second: does your personal travel list count it? These have entirely different answers, and mixing them up causes endless confusion in travel forums.

section_contexts.heading: The immigration answer is simple
section_contexts.summary: If you cleared passport control and entered the country, you were there. Full stop. That day counts toward any applicable visa limit, whether it's a Schengen 90/180 window, a US ESTA, or a tourist visa for Thailand. You physically entered the country's territory, your passport got stamped, and the clock started ticking.
detail_points: If you stayed airside — meaning you remained in the international transit zone of the airport and never went through border control — most countries do not count it as an entry. You were technically on sovereign territory (airports aren't extraterritorial, despite a common myth), but you did not formally enter the country under immigration law.

"Airside" means the area after security but before passport control on arrival. You might walk through duty-free shops, eat at airport restaurants, and sit at a gate for hours, but if you never approached an immigration counter and got your passport checked, you stayed in transit. Many large international airports have dedicated transit zones for exactly this purpose.

section_contexts.heading: The personal list is more complicated
section_contexts.summary: Travel collectors have debated this for decades, and nobody has settled it. The main schools of thought break down roughly like this:
detail_points: None of these positions is objectively right or wrong. But in practice, most serious travelers land somewhere in the moderate camp. If you cleared immigration and were physically in the country — even if you didn't leave the airport — that feels meaningfully different from cruising overhead at 35,000 feet. You interacted with the country's border system, you were subject to its laws, and you could have walked out the door.

The strict position has its appeal, but it creates awkward edge cases. What if you spent a full day exploring a city on a long layover? That seems to count under any definition. What if you spent three hours at the airport bar after immigration? The strict camp would say no, but you were undeniably in the country.

Strict: you left the airport, spent meaningful time in the country, saw something beyond the terminal. A two-hour connection where you cleared immigration but sat in the arrivals hall doesn't count. You need to have actually experienced the place.
Moderate: you cleared immigration and set foot on the country's soil, even if you only had a few hours during a connection. You were physically there, you went through border control, it counts.
Lenient: your plane landed there. Wheels on the tarmac. That's enough.

section_contexts.heading: The "did you experience it?" test
section_contexts.summary: Some travelers use a practical shorthand: did you eat a meal outside the airport, sleep a night, or do something in the country itself? If yes to any of those, it counts. This filters out pure connections while including long layovers where you actually ventured out.
detail_points: It's a reasonable middle ground. It's also ultimately arbitrary. What if you ate an incredible bowl of laksa at a hawker stall inside Changi Airport? You experienced something distinctly Singaporean without ever entering Singapore. What if you slept at an airside transit hotel? The lines blur quickly.
The honest answer is that no test will draw a clean boundary. Pick the one that feels right to you and apply it consistently.

section_contexts.heading: What about transit-only airports?
section_contexts.summary: Some airports are specifically engineered as connection hubs. Istanbul, Dubai, Singapore Changi, Doha Hamad — these airports handle millions of transit passengers who never intend to enter the country. You can spend 8 hours in Changi's butterfly garden, nap in a free rest area, take a shower, and eat Singaporean food without ever clearing immigration into Singapore.
detail_points: Is that visiting Singapore? You were physically in a building that sits on Singaporean land. You experienced something uniquely Singaporean. But you never entered Singapore in any legal or practical sense. The line is genuinely blurry, and anyone who tells you there's an obvious answer is oversimplifying.

Dubai takes this even further — the airport's duty-free zone is essentially a shopping mall. Some travelers spend more time in Dubai's airside transit area than they do in small countries they "officially" visited.

section_contexts.heading: How AtlasDays handles it
section_contexts.summary: The app doesn't decide for you. When you log a trip, you can mark it as a regular visit or as an airside transit . Both show up on your map, but transit trips appear in a different color so the distinction is visible at a glance.
detail_points: More importantly, transit trips don't count toward day totals or visa trackers . If you transited through Frankfurt without clearing Schengen immigration, that day doesn't eat into your 90/180 calculation. Your map still shows you were routed through Germany — because you were — but the numbers stay honest.
Your map, your rules. But the numbers that matter for immigration stay accurate regardless of how you categorize things personally.

section_contexts.heading: Our take
section_contexts.summary: If you cleared immigration, you visited. If you stayed airside, you transited. These are meaningfully different experiences, and pretending otherwise — in either direction — doesn't serve you.
detail_points: Record both. Label them differently. The people who get into trouble are the ones who don't record transits at all and then can't remember whether they actually entered a country or just connected through its airport. Three years later, when you're applying for a visa and need to list every country you've visited, you'll be glad you tracked the distinction.
Don't lose sleep over which camp you fall into. Just be consistent, be honest, and keep a record.
How Many Countries Are There in the World?
Country Counting Rules: Every Major List Compared
What Counts as a 'Day' for Visa Purposes?

articles.title: How to Export Your Travel History for a Visa Application
articles.headline: How to Export Your Travel History for a Visa Application
articles.description: Use AtlasDays' CSV export to compile an accurate travel history — ready for visa forms, immigration interviews, and consulate appointments.
articles.intro: Use AtlasDays' CSV export to compile an accurate travel history — ready for visa forms, immigration interviews, and consulate appointments.

section_contexts.heading: Why visa applications need your travel history
section_contexts.summary: Almost every major visa application asks for a detailed account of your travel history. Schengen visa forms typically request your travel over the past three years. US visa applications (DS-160) ask for every country you have visited in the last five years. UK and Australian visa forms may ask for travel going back a full decade. These are not casual questions — consular officers use your answers to evaluate your application.
detail_points: Immigration authorities are looking for patterns. They want to see whether you travel frequently and return home on time, whether you have a history of long stays that push up against visa limits, and whether there are any gaps or inconsistencies that might suggest an overstay. A well-documented travel history signals that you are an organized, low-risk applicant who respects immigration rules.

An incomplete or inaccurate travel history, on the other hand, can raise red flags. If your stated travel dates do not match your passport stamps, or if you omit a country that shows up in immigration databases, the consulate may request additional documentation, delay your application, or deny it outright. The stakes are real, and accuracy matters.

section_contexts.heading: The usual pain of compiling travel history
section_contexts.summary: When most people sit down to fill out a visa application, they realize they have no single source of truth for their travel history. The result is a scramble across multiple sources, none of which tell the complete story.
detail_points: You might start by flipping through your current passport, squinting at entry and exit stamps that are often faded, overlapping, or partially illegible. If you have traveled on a previous passport that has since expired, you need to dig that out too — assuming you still have it. Many countries no longer stamp passports at all, which means some trips leave no physical trace.

Next comes the email search: airline booking confirmations, hotel reservations, travel insurance policies. These can help you pin down dates, but they are scattered across years of inbox history and may not reflect actual travel dates if plans changed. A booked flight does not prove you took it, and a hotel confirmation does not tell you when you crossed the border.

section_contexts.heading: How AtlasDays makes this easy
section_contexts.summary: If you have been logging your trips in AtlasDays, the hard part is already done. Every time you record a trip in the app, you capture the country , the entry date , and the exit date . Over time, this builds into a complete, chronological travel history that lives in one place and is always up to date.
detail_points: When a visa application asks for your travel over the past five years, you do not need to reconstruct anything from memory. In AtlasDays, the information is already there — every country, every date, every duration. You can scroll through your history, verify it against your passport stamps if you want an extra layer of confidence, and transfer the details directly onto your application form.

This is especially valuable for people who travel frequently for work or who split time between multiple countries. The more trips you take, the harder it becomes to keep track manually — and the more important it becomes to have a reliable record. AtlasDays is meant to turn what would otherwise be hours of detective work into a straightforward lookup.

section_contexts.heading: Exporting to CSV
section_contexts.summary: Beyond just viewing your travel history in the app, AtlasDays can export your complete travel history as a CSV file . This gives you a clean, structured spreadsheet that you can use however you need.
detail_points: The export process is straightforward:

The resulting CSV file includes columns for country , entry date , exit date , and duration of stay . You can open it in Excel , Google Sheets , Apple Numbers , or any other spreadsheet application. From there, you can sort, filter, or format the data to match the specific requirements of your visa application.
Open AtlasDays on your iPhone or iPad.
Navigate to your tracker that contains the trips you want to export.
Tap the export option to generate your CSV file.

section_contexts.heading: What to include in your travel history
section_contexts.summary: Different visa applications have different requirements, but there are general expectations that apply across most consulates and immigration authorities:
detail_points: Having your travel history in a CSV export makes all of this significantly easier. You can see at a glance whether there are gaps, whether dates look right, and whether you have covered every trip the application period requires.

List all countries visited , not just the country you are applying to. If the form asks for your travel history over the past five years, that means every international trip — business, tourism, transit, and layovers that involved clearing immigration.

Use exact dates . "Sometime in June 2024" is not acceptable. Visa forms ask for specific entry and exit dates, and the expectation is that you provide them accurately. If your dates do not match the stamps in your passport, you may be asked to explain the discrepancy.

Present trips in chronological order . Most visa forms expect your travel history to be listed from the most recent trip backward, though some ask for chronological order from oldest to newest. Check the form instructions.

Account for gaps . If there are long periods where you did not travel, that is fine — but if there is a gap that does not make sense given the rest of your pattern (for example, a missing month between two trips that should have been consecutive), it may prompt questions.

section_contexts.heading: Building the habit
section_contexts.summary: The CSV export is only as good as the data behind it. If you start logging trips today but have three years of unrecorded travel before that, you will still need to reconstruct those earlier trips manually. The real value of AtlasDays comes from building the habit of logging every trip as it happens.
detail_points: The best approach is simple: when you arrive in a new country, log your entry . When you leave, log your exit . It takes a few seconds each time, and over the course of months and years, you build a complete, trustworthy record that requires no future effort to compile.

For travelers who apply for visas regularly — whether for work, family visits, or extended travel — this habit pays for itself many times over. Instead of spending hours before each application trying to piece together your history, you open the app, export the file, and move on. The time savings compound with every trip you take and every visa you apply for.

articles.title: How Many Countries Are There in the World?
articles.headline: How Many Countries Are There in the World?
articles.description: There are 195 recognized countries, but the real number depends on who's counting. Here's how the UN, ISO, FIFA, and travelers each count differently — and why it matters.
articles.intro: The answer depends on who's counting — and it matters for travelers.

section_contexts.heading: The short answer: 195
section_contexts.summary: The most commonly cited number is 195 countries . That comes from the 193 member states of the United Nations, plus two permanent observer states: Vatican City (the Holy See) and Palestine. This is the number you'll find in most textbooks, on most websites, and in most bar arguments.
detail_points: But if you're a traveler, 195 is incomplete. It leaves out places you can fly to, get a passport stamp in, and spend weeks exploring — places that feel like entirely separate countries even if they aren't politically recognized as such.

section_contexts.heading: Why the number varies
section_contexts.summary: There is no single authority that decides what counts as a "country." Different organizations maintain different lists, each built for a different purpose. Here are the major ones:
detail_points: Same planet, wildly different numbers. The "right" count depends entirely on what you're counting and why.

United Nations: 193 + 2 = 195. The UN recognizes 193 member states plus 2 observer states (Vatican City and Palestine). This is the diplomatic baseline. It doesn't include Taiwan, Kosovo, or Western Sahara — all of which are contested but function as distinct entities.

ISO 3166: 249 codes. The International Organization for Standardization assigns country codes for everything from shipping labels to domain names. Their list includes territories, dependencies, and special areas — so Guam (GU), Hong Kong (HK), and the British Virgin Islands (VG) all get their own codes.

FIFA: 211 members. Football's governing body accepts members that aren't UN-recognized states. England, Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland each field their own team. So do Gibraltar, Kosovo, and the Faroe Islands.

Olympics: 206 NOCs. The International Olympic Committee recognizes 206 National Olympic Committees. Athletes from Puerto Rico, Guam, the US Virgin Islands, and Chinese Taipei (Taiwan) compete under their own flags.

section_contexts.heading: What travelers actually care about
section_contexts.summary: For most people tracking their travels, the question isn't about political recognition. It's simpler than that: did I go somewhere meaningfully different?
detail_points: Hong Kong has its own currency, language mix, legal system, and immigration control. It feels nothing like Shenzhen across the border. Curaçao has its own flag, its own language (Papiamentu), and its own immigration stamps — but it's technically part of the Kingdom of the Netherlands. Greenland is geographically enormous and culturally distinct from Denmark, but it isn't a sovereign state.

The same goes for places like the Faroe Islands (self-governing under Denmark, with their own language and parliament), French Polynesia (autonomous territory of France, 15 hours from Paris by flight), and Bermuda (a British Overseas Territory with its own currency, 3,500 miles from London). These aren't just administrative subdivisions. They're places with their own character, their own entry requirements, and their own passport stamps.

section_contexts.heading: The territory question
section_contexts.summary: Should Guam count? What about Aruba, the Faroe Islands, or Macau? What about Bonaire, the Cook Islands, or Reunion? There's no universally correct answer, and reasonable travelers disagree.
detail_points: Some people are strict sovereigntists: if it doesn't have a seat at the UN, it doesn't count. Others take a more practical view: if it has its own immigration policy, its own culture, and you had to make a deliberate effort to get there, it counts.

Then there are the edge cases that make everyone argue. Does a layover count? Does driving across a border for lunch count? What about a cruise port where you spent three hours in the terminal? These are personal decisions, and the "right" answer is whichever one you can defend at dinner.

section_contexts.heading: What AtlasDays uses
section_contexts.summary: Rather than forcing one definition on everyone, AtlasDays lets you choose your own counting mode:
detail_points: You pick which mode matters to you. Your map highlights everything you've visited regardless of which counting mode is active — switching modes just changes your headline number, not your travel history.
UN 195: The standard 193 UN member states plus Vatican City and Palestine. Clean, simple, universally understood.

UN 195+2: Adds Kosovo and Taiwan to the list. Both are widely traveled destinations with their own airports, visas, and immigration systems — but neither holds a UN seat. This mode bumps the total to 197.

All Places: Includes territories, dependencies, and overseas regions — 325+ destinations in total. Hong Kong, Puerto Rico, the Faroe Islands, Aruba, French Polynesia, and dozens more. This is the mode for travelers who want credit for everywhere they've actually been.

section_contexts.heading: Why it matters for day counting
section_contexts.summary: If you're tracking visa days or tax residence, the political definition of "country" becomes very practical. Immigration rules are tied to sovereignty and international agreements, not to how a place "feels."
detail_points: The Schengen Area is the most obvious example: 29 European countries share a single visa counter. Spend 30 days in France and 30 days in Germany, and you've used 60 of your 90 allowed Schengen days — even though you visited two different countries. But hop to Ireland (EU, not Schengen) and your Schengen clock stops.

Overseas territories often have their own immigration rules that differ from their parent country. French Guiana is technically part of France and the EU, but Martinique has different entry rules for some nationalities. The British Virgin Islands and the Cayman Islands each have their own visa policies, separate from the UK. Curaçao and Aruba both belong to the Kingdom of the Netherlands, but neither is part of the Schengen Area.
Country Counting Rules: Every Major List Compared
Does a Layover Count as Visiting a Country?
What Counts as a 'Day' for Visa Purposes?

articles.title: How to Track Your Travel Days (And Why Spreadsheets Fail)
articles.headline: How to Track Your Travel Days (And Why Spreadsheets Fail)
articles.description: Why tracking travel days in a spreadsheet breaks down, and what to use instead for visa limits, tax residency, and travel history.
articles.intro: Why tracking travel days in a spreadsheet breaks down, and what to use instead for visa limits, tax residency, and travel history.

section_contexts.heading: Why travel day tracking matters
section_contexts.summary: If you travel internationally with any regularity, counting your days abroad is not optional — it is a legal requirement in many situations. Visa compliance is the most obvious reason: overstay your allowed days in a country or zone like the Schengen Area, and you risk fines, deportation, or future entry bans.
detail_points: But visas are only part of the picture. Tax residency rules in most countries are triggered by the number of days you spend there. Cross a threshold — often 183 days in a calendar year — and you may owe taxes in that country, sometimes in addition to your home country. Getting this wrong can mean surprise tax bills, penalties, or double taxation.

Then there are immigration forms . Many countries ask you to declare your travel history when applying for visas, residency permits, or citizenship. Having accurate records saves hours of guesswork and reduces the risk of inconsistencies that raise red flags with immigration officers.

section_contexts.heading: The spreadsheet approach
section_contexts.summary: Most people start with a spreadsheet. The setup seems straightforward: a column for the country, one for entry date, one for exit date, and a formula to calculate the number of days. Add a few conditional sums and you have a basic travel log.
detail_points: For a handful of trips per year to a single country, this works well enough. You can see your history at a glance, total up days per country, and feel reasonably confident in the numbers. It is simple, flexible, and free.

section_contexts.heading: Where spreadsheets break down
section_contexts.summary: The problems start as soon as your travel patterns get even slightly complicated.
detail_points: The real risk: spreadsheet errors are silent. You will not get a warning when your formula is wrong or when you have forgotten a trip. You only find out when a border officer tells you that you have overstayed.

Rolling windows are hard to formula. Rules like the Schengen 90/180-day limit use a rolling window, not a calendar period. Building a formula that looks back exactly 180 days from every possible date and sums your presence days is technically possible, but fragile and difficult to verify.

Multiple overlapping rules. If you need to track Schengen days, UK Standard Visitor limits, and US Substantial Presence Test days simultaneously, your spreadsheet becomes a tangle of tabs and cross-references.
Forgetting to update. A spreadsheet only works if you remember to open it and log every trip. Miss one entry and your counts are silently wrong — sometimes for months.

Timezone edge cases. Did you land before or after midnight? Entry and exit days can shift depending on the timezone of the country versus the timezone you departed from. Spreadsheets do not handle this gracefully.

section_contexts.heading: What a good travel tracker needs
section_contexts.summary: If you are going to move beyond a spreadsheet, the replacement should solve the specific problems spreadsheets create:
detail_points: Automatic day counting. Enter your trips once and let the tool calculate days per country, per zone, and per rule — including both entry and exit days where required.
Rolling window support. The tool should natively understand rolling windows like the Schengen 90/180 rule and show you exactly how many days you have remaining on any given date.
Per-country history. You should be able to see your complete travel history for any country at a glance, without filtering and sorting a massive table.
Offline access. Travel tracking is most useful at borders and airports — places where you may not have reliable internet. Your data should be available offline.
Private data. Your travel history is sensitive personal information. It should be stored on your device, not uploaded to a server you do not control.

section_contexts.heading: Spreadsheet vs. dedicated app
section_contexts.summary: A spreadsheet gives you full control and costs nothing. For simple annual trips to one or two countries, it may be all you need. But the moment you are managing rolling visa windows, tracking days across multiple regions, or traveling frequently enough that missed entries become likely, a dedicated travel tracker pays for itself in peace of mind.
detail_points: The tradeoff is straightforward: spreadsheets are flexible but manual . A purpose-built app removes the manual work and the risk of silent errors, at the cost of being more opinionated about how your data is structured.
AtlasDays tracks your travel days, visa windows, and residency thresholds automatically.

What Counts as a 'Day' for Visa Purposes?
183-Day Tax Residency Rule: What Travelers Need to Know

articles.title: How to Use AtlasDays to Track Your Visa Days
articles.headline: How to Use AtlasDays to Track Your Visa Days
articles.description: A getting-started guide to setting up trackers, logging trips, and staying on top of your visa limits with AtlasDays.
articles.intro: A getting-started guide to setting up trackers, logging trips, and staying on top of your visa limits.

section_contexts.heading: What AtlasDays does
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays is an iOS app designed to track visa day limits automatically. If you travel internationally and need to stay within a fixed number of allowed days — whether that is the Schengen Area's 90/180-day rolling window, a US B1/B2 stay, or a calendar-year cap in countries like Colombia — AtlasDays handles the counting for you.
detail_points: The app comes with a built-in Schengen tracker that implements the 90/180-day rolling window correctly out of the box. For every other country or visa rule, you can create a custom tracker that matches the specific rule type you need. Each tracker runs independently, so you can monitor multiple countries at the same time without any overlap or confusion between them.

Instead of maintaining spreadsheets or doing manual date arithmetic, you log your entry and exit dates and let the app calculate the rest. It tells you how many days you have used, how many remain, and when your limits reset — all updated instantly as you add trips.

section_contexts.heading: The Schengen tracker
section_contexts.summary: The Schengen 90/180-day rule is one of the most common — and most confusing — visa limits travelers encounter. You are allowed to spend up to 90 days within any rolling 180-day period inside the Schengen Area. Because the window rolls forward with each passing day, figuring out your remaining days by hand is notoriously error-prone.
detail_points: AtlasDays includes a dedicated Schengen tracker that is ready to use from the moment you open the app . There is nothing to configure. Simply log your entry and exit dates for any Schengen country — France, Germany, Spain, the Netherlands, or any of the other member states — and the app calculates your position within the rolling window automatically.
The Schengen tracker shows you three key pieces of information at all times:
Days used — how many of your 90 allowed days you have spent within the current 180-day window.
Days remaining — how many days you can still spend in the Schengen Area right now without exceeding the limit.
Next full stay — the earliest date when a full 90-day stay becomes available again, so you can plan longer future trips with confidence.

section_contexts.heading: Custom trackers
section_contexts.summary: The Schengen Area is just one of many countries and regions with day-count rules. The United States, the United Kingdom, Colombia, Thailand, and dozens of other destinations impose their own limits, and the rules vary widely. AtlasDays lets you create a custom tracker for any of them.
detail_points: When you create a custom tracker, you choose the rule type that matches the country's visa policy:

After choosing the rule type, set the day limit (and the window length, if applicable), give your tracker a name, and you are ready to start logging trips. The tracker counts down automatically based on the rule you selected.

Rolling window — Use this for rules that follow the pattern "X days in any Y-day period." This is the same logic the Schengen tracker uses, but you can set your own values. For example, the UK Standard Visitor visa informally follows a pattern where spending close to 180 days in any 12-month period can raise red flags at the border. A rolling-window tracker with a 180-day limit and a 365-day window captures this.

Calendar year — Use this for rules that reset on January 1 each year. Colombia, for example, allows tourists to stay up to 180 days per calendar year (typically granted in two 90-day periods). A calendar-year tracker counts your days from January through December and resets automatically when the new year begins.

Per-stay — Use this for rules that limit each individual visit rather than an aggregate period. The US Visa Waiver Program (ESTA), for instance, allows 90 days per entry . A per-stay tracker counts down from your entry date so you know exactly when your authorized stay expires.

section_contexts.heading: Logging a trip
section_contexts.summary: Adding a trip to any tracker takes just a few seconds. Open the tracker you want to update and tap to add a new trip. You will enter two pieces of information:
detail_points: You can also add an optional note to each trip — useful for recording a visa type, flight number, port of entry, or anything else you want to remember later.

As soon as you save the trip, your tracker updates instantly. The remaining days recalculate, and you can see the impact of the new trip right away. If you make a mistake, you can edit or delete any trip at any time — the tracker recalculates every time you make a change.
Entry date — the day you arrived in the country or region.
Exit date — the day you departed. If you are still there, you can leave this open and the app will count up to the current day.

section_contexts.heading: Staying informed
section_contexts.summary: The AtlasDays home screen gives you an at-a-glance overview of all your active trackers. Each tracker displays your remaining days and includes a visual indicator that shows how close you are to your limit. When you have plenty of days left, the indicator is calm. As you approach your limit, it shifts to signal that you are running low — so you never have to guess whether you are cutting it close.
detail_points: This overview is particularly valuable if you travel to multiple regions. Instead of switching between spreadsheets or apps, you see your Schengen balance, your US days, your UK days, and anything else you track, all in one place. Each tracker operates independently with its own rule type and limits, but they are all visible together so you can plan trips across destinations without losing track of any single one.

section_contexts.heading: iCloud sync
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays uses iCloud to sync your data across all your Apple devices. Log a trip on your iPhone during your travels, and it appears on your iPad when you get home — no manual transfer, no account setup, and no extra steps required.
detail_points: Because the app relies on iCloud rather than a custom server, your data never leaves Apple's ecosystem . There is no AtlasDays account to create, no email to register, and no personal information sent to third-party servers. Your trip data stays in your private iCloud storage, protected by whatever security measures you already have in place for your Apple ID.
Sync happens automatically in the background. As long as your devices are signed into the same Apple ID and have iCloud enabled, everything stays up to date without any intervention on your part.
Schengen 90/180-Day Rule Explained
How the Schengen Rolling Window Actually Works
How to Track Your Travel Days (And Why Spreadsheets Fail)
