articles.title: iCloud Sync for Travel Tracking: Why Your Data Follows You
articles.headline: iCloud Sync for Travel Tracking: Why Your Data Follows You
articles.description: How AtlasDays uses iCloud to keep your travel data in sync across devices — with no accounts, no servers, and no data leaving Apple's ecosystem.
articles.intro: How AtlasDays uses iCloud to keep your travel data in sync across devices — with no accounts, no servers, and no data leaving Apple's ecosystem.

section_contexts.heading: The problem with spreadsheets and notes
section_contexts.summary: Most travelers who take visa day limits seriously end up building some kind of tracking system. Maybe it is a Google Sheet with formulas that count days within rolling windows. Maybe it is a note in Apple Notes with a running list of entry and exit dates. Maybe it is a paper notebook tucked into a passport cover. These approaches all share the same fundamental weakness: they live in one place, and your life does not.
detail_points: The fragmentation problem becomes obvious at the worst possible moment. You are standing at an airport check-in counter and need to confirm how many days you have spent in the Schengen Area this period. Your spreadsheet is on your laptop, which is already packed in your checked bag. The note you kept on your phone has not been updated since your last trip because you made the most recent edits on your iPad at the hotel. The paper notebook is thorough but requires you to do mental arithmetic under pressure.

Even if you are disciplined about updating every device, manual sync is inherently fragile. You update the spreadsheet on your laptop, then forget to copy the changes to your phone. Or you add a trip on your phone and plan to transfer it later, but later never comes. Over time, you end up with multiple versions of your travel history, none of which you fully trust. When the stakes are visa compliance and potential overstay penalties, "pretty sure this is up to date" is not good enough.

section_contexts.heading: How AtlasDays syncs with iCloud
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays stores all of your travel data in iCloud using Apple's CloudKit framework. CloudKit is the same technology that Apple uses to sync your own apps like Photos, Notes, and Reminders across devices. When you log a trip on your iPhone, that data is written to your personal iCloud account and propagated to every other device signed into the same Apple ID. There is no delay, no manual export, and no "sync" button to press.
detail_points: The sync happens automatically in the background. Open AtlasDays on your iPad after logging a trip on your iPhone, and the trip is already there. Edit a date on one device, and the correction appears on the other within moments. This is not a feature you need to configure or enable — it is how the app works by default, as long as you are signed into iCloud on your devices.

Because AtlasDays uses CloudKit's private database, your travel data is stored in your personal iCloud account — not on servers owned or operated by AtlasDays. The app never sees, processes, or stores your data on any third-party infrastructure. From a data flow perspective, your information moves between your device and Apple's iCloud servers, and nowhere else.

section_contexts.heading: No account needed
section_contexts.summary: Most productivity and travel apps require you to create an account before you can use them. That means choosing a username, entering your email address, setting a password, and then verifying your email before you can even start. Some apps add further friction with mandatory onboarding flows, profile setup screens, or requests to connect social accounts. All of this stands between you and the thing you actually want to do: track your travel days.
detail_points: AtlasDays skips all of that. The app relies entirely on your Apple ID for identity and data storage. If you are signed into iCloud on your device — which virtually every iPhone and iPad user already is — sync just works. There is no signup flow, no password to remember, no email verification step, and no account recovery process to worry about.

This also means one fewer account to manage, one fewer password to store in your password manager, and one fewer service that has your email address. For travelers who already juggle dozens of accounts for airlines, hotels, visa services, and government portals, eliminating yet another login is a meaningful reduction in complexity.

section_contexts.heading: Privacy by design
section_contexts.summary: Travel data is more sensitive than most people realize. Your entry and exit dates reveal where you have been, how long you stayed, and how frequently you travel. Combined with passport country information, this data paints a detailed picture of your movements and immigration status. It is not the kind of information you want sitting on a startup's server, subject to their security practices and data retention policies.
detail_points: With AtlasDays, your travel data never touches third-party servers . It lives in two places: on your device and in your iCloud account. Both are encrypted by Apple — data is encrypted in transit and at rest. AtlasDays has no analytics SDK, no tracking pixels, no crash reporting that sends your data to external services, and no way for anyone at AtlasDays to access or view your travel history.

This is not a privacy policy promise that could change with the next update or acquisition. It is an architectural decision. Because the app uses CloudKit's private database, there is no mechanism for the developer to query your data even if they wanted to. The privacy guarantee is enforced by Apple's infrastructure, not by a policy document.

section_contexts.heading: Why this beats spreadsheets
section_contexts.summary: Spreadsheets are powerful, but they were not designed for travel day tracking. To calculate a rolling 180-day window in Google Sheets, you need to write formulas that count days within a moving date range, account for partial stays, and handle the edge cases where trips span window boundaries. Most travelers who attempt this end up with formulas they do not fully understand and cannot easily verify.
detail_points: Beyond the formula complexity, spreadsheets have practical limitations for this use case:

AtlasDays handles all of this automatically. You log your trips — entry date, exit date, country — and the app calculates your remaining days for each visa rule you are tracking. The calculations update in real time, the data syncs across devices without intervention, and you get a clear answer without needing to audit a formula chain.
No automatic calculations: Every new trip requires you to manually enter dates and verify that your formulas still produce correct results. One mistyped date can throw off your entire count.
No alerts: A spreadsheet will not warn you when you are approaching a visa limit. You have to remember to check it, and you have to trust your formulas are correct when you do.

Unreliable cross-device access: Google Sheets works on phones, but the mobile experience is clunky for data entry. Apple Numbers does not sync as reliably between devices. Excel files require manual transfers.

section_contexts.heading: Multi-device scenarios
section_contexts.summary: The value of seamless sync becomes clear when you look at how travelers actually use their devices throughout a trip. Real-world usage rarely happens on just one device, and the moments when you need your travel data most are often the moments when your preferred device is not at hand.
detail_points: In each of these cases, the key benefit is the same: you never have to think about whether the device in your hand has the latest version of your data. It always does.
iCloud sync requires an Apple ID and an active iCloud account. AtlasDays is launching soon for iPhone and iPad.

Logging a trip at the airport: You have just cleared immigration and want to record your entry date while it is fresh in your mind. Your iPhone is in your pocket. You open AtlasDays, add the entry, and it is done. Later that evening, when you open the app on your iPad at the hotel, the trip is already there.

Reviewing your Schengen days at the hotel: You are planning the rest of your Europe itinerary and want to see exactly how many days you have left in the Schengen Area. Your iPad gives you a comfortable view of your full travel timeline, and every trip you logged on your phone is already accounted for.

Checking remaining US days before booking a flight: You are browsing flights on your laptop and want to confirm you have enough days left on your US authorization before committing to travel dates. You pull out your phone, open AtlasDays, and get an instant answer — no need to find and open a spreadsheet file.

articles.title: How to Prove Time Spent in a Country When You Don't Have Perfect Records
articles.headline: How to Prove Time Spent in a Country When You Don't Have Perfect Records
articles.description: Learn how to prove time spent in a country when records are incomplete, using documents that support exact proof or a reasonable reconstruction.
articles.intro: Use a mix of direct records and supporting evidence to build a timeline that is clear about what is exact and what is reconstructed.

section_contexts.heading: Short answer
section_contexts.summary: You usually do not need one perfect document. What matters is whether your evidence supports the dates you are claiming. Exact proof is best, but a reasonable reconstruction is often enough when you label uncertainty honestly and keep the timeline consistent.

section_contexts.heading: Two levels of evidence
section_contexts.summary: It helps to separate records into two groups:
detail_points: Exact proof tells you when a trip definitely started or ended. Supporting evidence helps you fill gaps around it. A strong file usually uses both.
Do not treat every document as equal. A photo proves presence at a moment. A hotel invoice proves a stay. A card charge may only narrow the date window. Use each record for what it can actually show.
Exact proof: passport stamps, official entry and exit records, boarding passes, booking confirmations, or a digital arrival record.
Supporting evidence: bank statements, receipts, hotel invoices, mobile photos, message timestamps, calendar entries, and travel insurance documents.

section_contexts.heading: How to organize the evidence
section_contexts.summary: Make one line per trip. Keep each country or trip window separate.
detail_points: Attach the strongest record first. Use official records or stamps when you have them.
Add supporting documents in date order. Place bookings, receipts, photos, and charges beside the trip they support.
Mark exact and approximate dates differently. Do not mix confirmed dates with guessed ones.
Write a short note for any gap. If you are missing one leg of a trip, say why it is missing and what evidence fills the gap.

section_contexts.heading: Example template
section_contexts.summary: Use a structure like this when you need a clean reconstruction:

section_contexts.heading: What to say when you are uncertain
section_contexts.summary: If you cannot recover exact dates, say so plainly. Use phrases like approximate dates , month and year only , or best available reconstruction . That is better than presenting a false exact date that will not match the underlying records.

section_contexts.heading: Where AtlasDays fits
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays is useful once you start keeping the evidence together in one place. It gives you a travel timeline you can review later, so you are not rebuilding the same history from scratch every time a form asks for it.
detail_points: AtlasDays helps you preserve trips, notes, and supporting records in one timeline so your reconstruction stays organized.

articles.title: How to Rebuild Your Travel History From Passport Stamps, Emails, and Photos
articles.headline: How to Rebuild Your Travel History From Passport Stamps, Emails, and Photos
articles.description: Rebuild a reliable travel history from passport stamps, emails, photos, bookings, bank statements, and official records when your trip log is incomplete.
articles.intro: A practical way to reconstruct trips when your travel log is incomplete, scattered, or years out of date.
articles.plan_notes: Lean into reconstruction from scattered evidence: photos, tickets, emails, stamps, and notes becoming one coherent timeline.

section_contexts.heading: Short answer
section_contexts.summary: Start with the records you trust most, then work outward. Passport stamps, airline emails, hotel bookings, card statements, and photo timestamps can usually be combined into a usable timeline. The goal is not to make every trip perfect. The goal is to create a chronology that is defensible, clearly labeled, and easy to verify later.

section_contexts.heading: What to gather first
section_contexts.summary: Look for sources that anchor exact dates before you fill in the gaps:
detail_points: Do not start by guessing. Build from evidence first. If you estimate too early, you usually end up having to rewrite the whole timeline later.
Passport stamps for entry and exit dates, recognizing that some countries stamp inconsistently or not at all.
Email confirmations from airlines, hotels, trains, and car rentals.
Bank and card statements that show foreign spending on or near the travel dates.
Photo timestamps that can confirm where you were on a given day or during a specific week.

section_contexts.heading: A simple reconstruction workflow
section_contexts.summary: Make a master list of trips. Create one row per trip, even if the dates are incomplete.
detail_points: Anchor each trip with the strongest record. Use stamps, bookings, or official travel records first.
Fill the gaps with supporting evidence. Match hotel stays, bank activity, and photo timestamps to the same trip window.
Label uncertainty honestly. If you only know the month and year, say so. If you only know the approximate week, mark it as approximate.
Separate exact from estimated entries. Keep a clean distinction between confirmed dates and reconstructed dates.

section_contexts.heading: How to handle missing stamps
section_contexts.summary: Missing stamps are normal. Some countries do not stamp every entry or exit, and some stamps are too faint to read. If that happens, use the nearest surrounding evidence to narrow the trip window:
detail_points: A flight receipt can confirm departure and arrival cities.
A hotel invoice can prove you were present on a certain night.
A bank charge can show the country and approximate date.
Photo metadata can confirm you were already in-country.

section_contexts.heading: What a clean timeline should look like
section_contexts.summary: A final version should read like a record, not a memory dump. Each line should include the country, the best-known entry and exit dates, and a note about what the dates are based on. If a trip is approximate, say that directly instead of pretending it is exact.
detail_points: For example: France, 2024-05-11 to 2024-05-18, based on flight confirmation and hotel receipts . Or: Portugal, early June 2023, based on passport stamp plus photo timestamps .
AtlasDays gives you one place to log trips, preserve evidence, and keep your history ready for the next form.

Travel History Template for Visa Applications

articles.title: Schengen 90/180-Day Rule Explained
articles.headline: Schengen 90/180-Day Rule Explained
articles.description: How the Schengen 90/180-day rule works, how to count your days correctly, and how to avoid overstaying with a rolling window calculator.
articles.intro: How the rolling window works, how to count your days, and how to avoid overstaying.
articles.plan_notes: Europe should be instantly recognizable. Emphasize the rolling-window idea more than passports or airport interiors.

section_contexts.heading: What is the 90/180-day rule?
section_contexts.summary: Non-EU citizens visiting the Schengen Area can stay for a maximum of 90 days within any 180-day rolling window . This applies across all 29 Schengen countries combined — days in France, Germany, and Spain all count toward the same 90-day limit.

section_contexts.heading: How the rolling window works
section_contexts.summary: The 180-day window is not a fixed period. It rolls forward with each day. On any given day, border officials look back 180 days and count how many of those days you spent in the Schengen Area. If it's 90 or more, you cannot enter.
detail_points: This is where most people get confused. It's not "90 days per half-year" — it's a continuous sliding window that moves with you.

The common mistake: thinking you can do "90 days in, 90 days out, repeat." This doesn't always work because the window is rolling, not fixed. Depending on your travel pattern, you may need to wait longer than 90 days before your full allowance resets.
You arrive in Spain on January 1 and leave on March 31 — that's 90 days used.
On April 1, you look back 180 days (to October 4 of the previous year). Your 90 days from Jan–Mar are all within this window, so you have 0 days remaining.
On April 2, October 5 drops out of the window — but that doesn't help because your first day (Jan 1) is still within the 180-day lookback.

section_contexts.heading: Which countries count?
section_contexts.summary: All 29 Schengen member states share the same 90/180 counter. Days in any of these countries are added together:
detail_points: Austria, Belgium, Bulgaria, Croatia, Czech Republic, Denmark, Estonia, Finland, France, Germany, Greece, Hungary, Iceland, Italy, Latvia, Liechtenstein, Lithuania, Luxembourg, Malta, Netherlands, Norway, Poland, Portugal, Romania, Slovakia, Slovenia, Spain, Sweden, and Switzerland.

section_contexts.heading: How to count your days correctly
section_contexts.summary: Doing this manually with a spreadsheet is possible but error-prone — especially with multiple short trips across different countries. Each new trip changes the math for every future date.
detail_points: Both entry and exit days count. If you arrive on the 1st and leave on the 3rd, that's 3 days — not 2.
Count across all Schengen countries combined , not per country. A week in France and a week in Italy is 14 days, not two separate 7-day counters.
Use the rolling method: for any given date, look back 180 days and total the days you were present.
Airport transits do not count if you stay in the international zone and do not clear immigration.

section_contexts.heading: Common scenarios that trip people up
section_contexts.summary: Multiple short trips: five weekend trips to different Schengen countries are easy to forget. Each one eats into the same 90-day allowance, and the rolling window means old days free up at unpredictable intervals.
detail_points: Trips that straddle the window boundary: if a trip started inside the 180-day window but ended outside it, only the days within the window count. This is where manual counting gets unreliable.

Non-Schengen EU countries: Ireland is in the EU but not in the Schengen Area. Days in Ireland do not count toward your 90-day Schengen limit. Cyprus and Romania's status can also cause confusion — both are now Schengen members as of recent years.

section_contexts.heading: What happens if you overstay?
section_contexts.summary: Consequences vary by country but can include fines, deportation, future visa refusals, and multi-year entry bans. Germany and the Netherlands are known for strict enforcement. Southern European countries may be more lenient in practice, but overstays are still recorded in the Schengen Information System and can affect future travel across all member states.
detail_points: Even a single day over the limit creates a record. The safest approach is to know your exact count before you travel, not after.

section_contexts.heading: Stop counting manually
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays lets you log your trips once and tracks your rolling 90/180 window automatically — no formulas, no guesswork.
detail_points: AtlasDays counts your rolling visa window so you don't have to.

articles.title: Schengen Countries List for the 90/180 Rule
articles.headline: Schengen Countries List for the 90/180 Rule
articles.description: The current Schengen countries list for the 90/180 rule, with a clear distinction between Schengen, EU, and non-Schengen countries.
articles.intro: The Schengen Area members that count together under the short-stay 90/180 rule.

section_contexts.heading: Short answer
section_contexts.summary: The Schengen 90/180 rule applies across the 29 countries in the Schengen Area . For short stays, your days are pooled across all of them, so time in France, Italy, and Spain all counts toward the same limit.
detail_points: That is why the country list matters: the rule is shared by the whole area, not reset every time you cross a border.

section_contexts.heading: Current Schengen countries
section_contexts.summary: Important distinction: not every EU country is in Schengen. Ireland is in the EU but not in Schengen. Cyprus is also an EU country, but it is not yet part of the Schengen short-stay system. The UK is outside Schengen entirely.

section_contexts.heading: What about Vatican City, San Marino, Monaco, and Andorra?
section_contexts.summary: These four micro-states are not Schengen members, but their geography means your days there can still count toward your 90-day limit:
detail_points: Vatican City — Completely surrounded by Italy. There is no border control between Italy and Vatican City, so entering Vatican City counts as being in Italy. Days there count toward your Schengen total.
San Marino — Also surrounded by Italy, with no border control. Same rule applies: time spent in San Marino counts as time in the Schengen Area.
Monaco — Bordered by France and the Mediterranean Sea. The open border with France means days in Monaco are treated as Schengen days in practice.

Andorra — Located between France and Spain, both of which are Schengen members. Andorra itself is not Schengen, and days spent entirely within Andorra do not count toward the 90-day limit. However, every time you enter or leave Andorra, you cross through France or Spain, which uses an entry and exit day on your Schengen count.

section_contexts.heading: Why this list matters
section_contexts.summary: If a country is in Schengen, its days count toward the same 90-day limit as every other Schengen country. A two-week trip split between Amsterdam, Berlin, and Barcelona still uses 14 Schengen days.
detail_points: If a country is not in Schengen, its stay rules are separate. That is why travelers often need to distinguish between EU membership and Schengen membership before they plan a trip.

section_contexts.heading: How to think about it in practice
section_contexts.summary: That simple habit prevents the most common mistake: treating the Schengen area like a collection of separate country counters when it is actually one shared pool.
detail_points: Check whether your destination is inside the Schengen Area, not just the EU.
Count all days spent inside any Schengen country together.
Use the 180-day rolling lookback when planning future entry dates.
Do not assume that crossing a border resets your allowance.

section_contexts.heading: Need the counting rule itself?
section_contexts.summary: If you want the day-counting math, read the main Schengen explainer and the rolling-window walkthrough next.
detail_points: AtlasDays helps you keep a clear record of where you have been and how many days you still have left.
Schengen 90/180-Day Rule Explained
How the Schengen Rolling Window Actually Works
What Counts as a "Day" for Visa Purposes?

articles.title: How the Schengen Rolling Window Actually Works
articles.headline: How the Schengen Rolling Window Actually Works
articles.description: A day-by-day walkthrough of the Schengen 90/180-day rolling window rule, with a realistic example showing exactly how the math works.
articles.intro: A day-by-day walkthrough of the 90/180-day rule — the same math the tracker does for you.

section_contexts.heading: The rule in one sentence
section_contexts.summary: You may stay in the Schengen Area for a maximum of 90 days within any rolling 180-day period , and this limit applies to all 29 Schengen member states combined. It does not matter whether you spend those 90 days in a single country or spread them across ten different ones — France, Germany, Spain, Italy, and every other Schengen state share the same counter. Once you have accumulated 90 days of presence within the current 180-day lookback window, you must leave and wait until enough days expire from the window before you can re-enter.

section_contexts.heading: What "rolling" really means
section_contexts.summary: This is where most people get confused. The 90/180 rule is not a simple "90 days on, 90 days off" system. There is no fixed period that resets on a calendar date. Instead, the 180-day window slides forward by one day, every single day .
detail_points: Here is how to think about it: on any given date, look back exactly 180 days. Count every day you were physically present in the Schengen Area during that 180-day window. If the total is 90 or fewer, you are within the limit. If it exceeds 90, you have overstayed.

Tomorrow, the window shifts forward by one day. The oldest day in today's window drops off the back, and a new day is added at the front. This means that a day you spent in the Schengen Area 181 days ago no longer counts against you — it has "rolled off" the window. This rolling mechanic is what makes the rule both flexible and genuinely difficult to calculate by hand.

section_contexts.heading: A day-by-day example
section_contexts.summary: Let's walk through a realistic scenario with specific dates.
detail_points: You arrive in Paris on January 15 and depart on February 13 . Both the entry day and the exit day count as days of presence. That gives you 30 days used (January 15 through February 13 inclusive).
You return to the Schengen Area, arriving in Amsterdam on April 1 and departing on May 15 . That is 45 days (April 1 through May 15 inclusive).
Trip 1 (Jan 15 - Feb 13): This entire trip falls before March 6, so zero days from Trip 1 are counted. All 30 days have rolled off the window.
Trip 2 (Apr 1 - May 15): This trip falls entirely within the window, so all 45 days are counted.
Trip 1 (Jan 15 - Feb 13): The entire trip falls within the window. That is 30 days .

section_contexts.heading: Common mistakes
section_contexts.summary: Even experienced travelers get tripped up by the rolling window. Here are the most frequent errors:
detail_points: If you stay for 90 days straight and then leave, you do not get a fresh 90 days after waiting 90 days. Because the window is 180 days, and you used 90 days at the start of it, those 90 days only begin to roll off one at a time starting 180 days after your first day of entry. You will gradually reclaim days, not receive them all at once.

A week in France, ten days in Italy, and two weeks in Spain are not tracked separately. They all draw from the same 90-day pool. Hopping between Schengen countries does not buy you extra time — there are no border checks between member states, and the rule treats the entire Schengen Area as a single zone.

section_contexts.heading: Why this is hard to do by hand
section_contexts.summary: If you only take one trip per year, the math is straightforward. But most people who worry about the 90/180 rule are making multiple trips — a business conference in Berlin, a vacation in Greece, a long weekend in Lisbon, a family visit in Vienna. Each trip adds a block of days to your rolling window, and each block ages off at a different time.
detail_points: To accurately check your status, you need to look at every single date from today back 180 days and determine whether you were inside or outside the Schengen Area on each one. If you have three or four trips in the window, you are juggling overlapping date ranges, counting inclusive days, and doing it all against a deadline that shifts daily.

One miscounted day — forgetting a layover in a Schengen airport, miscounting the days in a month, or overlooking the fact that entry and exit days both count — can push you from 89 to 91 days. That single day can result in an overstay on your record, fines at the border, or a ban on future Schengen entry.

section_contexts.heading: Set this up in AtlasDays
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays includes a Schengen tracker that performs this exact rolling-window calculation automatically. When you log your trips, the app looks back 180 days from today and counts your days of presence — the same math described in this article, but done instantly and without error.
detail_points: All you need to do is log your entry and exit dates. The app handles the rolling window, the inclusive day counting, and the multi-trip overlap. If you also travel to countries with other visa rules, each one gets its own tracker in the same place.

This article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute immigration or legal advice. Immigration rules change frequently. Consult a qualified immigration attorney or check official Schengen regulations for guidance on your specific situation.
Days used in the current 180-day window
Days remaining before you hit the 90-day limit
Next available full-stay date — the earliest date you could enter and stay for a full 90 days

articles.title: How to Prepare Travel History for a Visa Application
articles.headline: How to Prepare Travel History for a Visa Application
articles.description: What visa officers look for in your travel history, how to compile it accurately, and how to present it on applications.
articles.intro: What visa officers look for, how to compile your history accurately, and how to present it without mistakes.

section_contexts.heading: Why visa applications ask for travel history
section_contexts.summary: Your travel history tells a visa officer more than just where you've been. It reveals patterns. Frequent, short trips with consistent returns home signal that you respect visa conditions and have strong ties to your home country. On the other hand, excessive stays in a single country, unexplained gaps, or a pattern of pushing visa limits can raise red flags.
detail_points: Officers use your travel record to assess risk. A well-documented history of international travel — with timely departures and a mix of destinations — builds credibility. A sparse or inconsistent record doesn't necessarily hurt you, but it gives the officer less evidence to work with when deciding in your favor.

section_contexts.heading: What they typically ask for
section_contexts.summary: Most visa applications request your travel history for the last 5 to 10 years . The specific details vary by country, but you should expect to provide:
detail_points: Some countries also ask whether you were refused entry or deported at any point, and whether you overstayed a previous visa. Answer these honestly — immigration databases are more connected than most people assume.
Countries visited — every country, not just the ones you think are relevant.
Dates of entry and exit — as precise as possible, ideally exact dates.
Purpose of each trip — tourism, business, visiting family, transit, study, etc.
Duration of stay — some forms calculate this from your dates, others ask you to state it.

section_contexts.heading: Where to find your own travel history
section_contexts.summary: Reconstructing years of travel from memory alone is unreliable. Use these sources instead:
detail_points: Passport stamps — the most obvious source, but not always complete. Some countries don't stamp on entry or exit, and stamps can be illegible.
Airline and hotel bookings — search your email for confirmation receipts. Airlines often keep booking records for several years.

Immigration records — the US stores entry and exit data in the I-94 system , which you can access online. The UK maintains digital immigration records. Schengen countries rely on entry and exit stamps, though the upcoming EES (Entry/Exit System) will digitize this.
Bank and credit card statements — foreign transactions can help you pin down dates when other records are missing.
Previous visa applications — if you've applied before, your earlier submissions may contain the travel history you already compiled.

section_contexts.heading: Common problems
section_contexts.summary: Even careful travelers run into issues when compiling their history:
detail_points: Keep your old passports. Even expired ones are valuable as a record of past travel. If you've already discarded one, check whether your country's immigration authority can provide a history of your movements.
Missing or illegible stamps — border agents don't always stamp clearly, and some countries have moved away from stamps entirely.
Gaps you can't account for — if you traveled frequently years ago without keeping records, some periods may be impossible to reconstruct precisely.
Discarded old passports — many people throw away expired passports without realizing they contain the only record of past travel.
Travel before biometric tracking — trips taken before countries implemented digital systems may have no electronic record at all.

section_contexts.heading: How to compile your history
section_contexts.summary: Create a chronological list with four columns: country, entry date, exit date, and purpose of visit. Work backward from your current passport and fill in details from the sources listed above.
detail_points: For trips where you can't determine exact dates, provide your best estimate and be transparent. Writing "approximately June 2017" or noting "exact dates unavailable for trips before 2018" is far better than fabricating precise dates. Visa officers understand that older travel is harder to document — what they don't tolerate is inconsistency or dishonesty.
If you traveled to multiple countries in a single trip (for example, a Europe trip covering France, Italy, and Spain), list each country separately with its own entry and exit dates.

section_contexts.heading: Tips for a clean application
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays keeps a country-by-country log so you're always ready when paperwork asks for travel history.
detail_points: Be consistent across applications. If you apply for visas to multiple countries, the travel history you provide should match. Discrepancies between applications — even innocent ones — can trigger additional scrutiny.
Don't omit countries. Leaving out a country because you think it's irrelevant, or because the visit was short, can backfire if the officer discovers it through database checks.

Keep a running log going forward. The easiest way to avoid scrambling before your next application is to record each trip as it happens — country, dates, and purpose — so the information is ready when you need it.
Double-check your dates against stamps. A one-day error might seem trivial, but if it makes your stay appear longer than the visa allowed, it could look like an overstay.
Include transit stops if asked. Some applications count transit through a country as a visit. Read the form's instructions carefully.

articles.title: Travel History Template for Visa Applications
articles.headline: Travel History Template for Visa Applications
articles.description: Use this travel history template for visa applications, with a copyable example table and a simple way to organize trip dates, countries, and notes.
articles.intro: Use this simple template to organize trip dates, countries, and notes before you fill in a visa form or prepare supporting documents.
articles.plan_notes: Favor tidy organization, timeline structure, and travel records. Avoid anything that looks like an official government form.

section_contexts.heading: The short answer
section_contexts.summary: The easiest travel history template is a chronological list with the same fields every time: country, entry date, exit date, purpose, and a note about where you found the information. That keeps your answers consistent and makes it easier to check your work before submitting an application.
detail_points: Different visa forms ask for different details, but the structure below works as a starting point for most applications. If a form asks for more or less, adapt the columns rather than inventing a new system each time.

section_contexts.heading: How to use the template
section_contexts.summary: List trips in chronological order so the timeline is easy to follow.
detail_points: Use exact dates whenever you can. If you are unsure, mark the entry as approximate instead of pretending it is exact.
Add one source for every trip, even if it is only a booking confirmation or email receipt.
Keep notes short and factual. The goal is to help the reviewer understand the trip, not to write a story.
Check for conflicts before submitting. Dates, countries, and purposes should line up across forms and attachments.

section_contexts.heading: What to include if your records are incomplete
section_contexts.summary: If you do not have perfect records, build the table from the best evidence you have. Passport stamps, airline emails, hotel confirmations, bank statements, photo timestamps, and official entry or exit records can all help reconstruct a trip.
detail_points: When a date is uncertain, write that it is estimated. That is better than guessing and later giving a different answer in another application.
Keep the same structure every time. A stable format makes your travel history easier to update, easier to verify, and easier to reuse for future visa forms.

section_contexts.heading: How AtlasDays fits in
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays keeps your trips organized as you go, so the same history you use for day counting can also be exported and reused when a visa application asks for travel details. That matters most when you travel often and do not want to rebuild the same table from scratch every time.
detail_points: AtlasDays helps you maintain a trip history you can reuse when forms ask for exact dates, destinations, and stay lengths.
How to Prepare Travel History for a Visa Application
How to Rebuild Your Travel History From Passport Stamps, Emails, and Photos
How to Prove Time Spent in a Country When You Don't Have Perfect Records

articles.title: UK ETA vs Standard Visitor Visa: What's the Difference?
articles.headline: UK ETA vs Standard Visitor Visa: What's the Difference?
articles.description: UK ETA and Standard Visitor visa are not the same. Learn which one applies, when ETA is enough, and when to check the visa route first.
articles.intro: If you're visiting the UK, the right answer depends on your nationality, your reason for travel, and whether you need a visa or ETA.

section_contexts.heading: The short answer
section_contexts.summary: An ETA is travel permission for eligible nationalities. A Standard Visitor visa is a visa . They are not the same thing, and neither one automatically guarantees entry at the border.
detail_points: In practice, many visitors will use an ETA if they are eligible, while others need a Standard Visitor visa instead. The correct route depends on your passport and the reason you are coming to the UK.
Do not treat an ETA as automatic entry. GOV.UK says an ETA does not guarantee entry to the UK. Border officers still decide whether you can enter when you arrive.

section_contexts.heading: When an ETA is usually the right route
section_contexts.summary: Use the ETA path if your nationality is eligible and you are coming for a permitted short visit, such as tourism, visiting friends or family, business travel, or short study. GOV.UK also notes that many visitors from Europe, the USA, Australia, Canada, and certain other countries usually need an ETA rather than a visa.
detail_points: If you already know you are eligible, the ETA route is simpler than a visa application. But you still need to check the official eligibility tool before relying on it.

section_contexts.heading: When you may need a visa instead
section_contexts.summary: Some people need a Standard Visitor visa instead of an ETA. The most obvious cases are people whose nationality is not ETA-eligible and people whose trip purpose falls outside the ETA route.
detail_points: GOV.UK also points out that if you have a criminal record or were previously refused entry to the UK, you may want to apply for a Standard Visitor visa instead. That is a high-level rule of thumb, not a substitute for checking the official route checker.

The Standard Visitor rules also cover cases where people need a visitor visa because they are applying from a visa-national country, or because their travel reason needs a specific visitor category rather than the ETA path.

section_contexts.heading: What the Standard Visitor route covers
section_contexts.summary: The Standard Visitor route is for temporary visits, usually up to 6 months, for activities such as tourism, visiting family, business trips, and short courses. That route is broader than the ETA flow, because it is the visitor visa category the immigration rules use for those who need a visa to visit.

section_contexts.heading: How to check the right answer
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays keeps your trips organized so visa questions, day counts, and return planning stay easy to verify.
detail_points: Start with the official GOV.UK visa checker .
Confirm whether your nationality can use an ETA.
Check whether your reason for travel fits the visitor route you want.
Read the Standard Visitor rules if you are unsure how long you can stay or what activities are allowed.
Standard Visitor guidance

articles.title: UK Standard Visitor: How Length of Stay Is Assessed
articles.headline: UK Standard Visitor: How Length of Stay Is Assessed
articles.description: How UK Standard Visitor stays are assessed, why there is no formal 180-day-in-12-month rule, and what border officers look for.
articles.intro: How the UK's visitor rules work, why there is no formal 180-day-in-12-month formula, and what border officers actually look for.

section_contexts.heading: What is the UK Standard Visitor route?
section_contexts.summary: The UK Standard Visitor route allows travel to the United Kingdom for tourism, family visits, business meetings, short courses of study, medical treatment, and a limited set of other visitor activities. A visit is usually limited to up to 6 months .
detail_points: Many nationalities can enter the UK without applying for a visitor visa in advance. Others need a Standard Visitor visa before travel. Either way, the same visitor rules apply when UK authorities decide whether you can enter and how long you may stay.
Unlike a work visa or residence permission, visitor status is for genuine visits. You are expected to leave at the end of the visit and not use repeated trips to make the UK your main home.

section_contexts.heading: Is there a formal "180 days in 12 months" rule?
section_contexts.summary: No. The UK visitor rules do not create a Schengen-style rolling window and do not say that you automatically fail after 180 days in any 12-month period. The legal rule is simpler: a Standard Visitor stay is usually limited to up to 6 months per visit.
detail_points: What makes the UK different is discretion. Border Force officers and Home Office caseworkers look at your overall travel pattern to decide whether you are still a genuine visitor . Frequent or successive visits can cause problems if they suggest that you are effectively living in the UK through repeated entries.

That is why people talk about an "unwritten 180-day rule." It is really a shorthand for enforcement risk, not a formal threshold written into the rules. Long visits, repeated returns after short absences, and weak ties outside the UK are what increase that risk.

section_contexts.heading: How it differs from the Schengen rule
section_contexts.summary: In the Schengen Area, you can count your remaining days precisely using the rolling 90/180-day window. The UK offers much less certainty. Key differences include:
detail_points: No fixed rolling window: Border officers use judgment, not a Schengen-style calculator.
More discretion: Officers assess your overall circumstances, including purpose of visit, finances, and ties outside the UK.
Genuine visitor test: The core question is whether you are visiting temporarily or effectively living in the UK through frequent or successive visits.

section_contexts.heading: Common reasons for refusal
section_contexts.summary: Understanding why people get refused helps you avoid the same mistakes. Common patterns that lead to refusal or heavy questioning at the UK border include:
detail_points: Frequent or successive long visits. Leaving the UK briefly and returning for another long stay can suggest that you are using visitor status to live there.
Weak evidence that you live elsewhere. If you cannot show strong ties outside the UK, officers may doubt that you plan to leave.
Work or residence patterns disguised as visits. Visitors cannot live in the UK for extended periods through repeated entries and cannot work except for narrow permitted activities.
Inconsistent explanations. If your stated purpose does not match your travel history, finances, or supporting documents, scrutiny rises quickly.

section_contexts.heading: Electronic Travel Authorisation (ETA)
section_contexts.summary: The UK is rolling out an Electronic Travel Authorisation (ETA) system for visitors who do not currently need a visa. Similar to the US ESTA or EU ETIAS, an ETA is a digital pre-travel permission linked to your passport. It does not replace the Standard Visitor rules — you still cannot exceed the 6-month limit per visit or use the UK as a place of residence.
detail_points: The ETA does, however, give the Home Office more data about travel patterns before you even board your flight. If your history shows excessive time in the UK, it could affect future ETA approvals.

section_contexts.heading: Tips for frequent UK visitors
section_contexts.summary: If you visit the UK regularly, a few habits will reduce the chance of problems at the border:
detail_points: Keep a clear record of your stays. Know your recent UK travel history before you fly.
Be prepared to explain your purpose. Have return travel, accommodation details, and proof of ties outside the UK ready if needed.
Do not treat 6 months as a target. A long stay is not automatically unlawful, but repeated long stays make the genuine visitor test harder to satisfy.
Avoid back-to-back patterns. Shorter, well-spaced visits are easier to defend than repeated near-maximum stays.
Be consistent. Your explanation, documents, and travel history should tell the same story.

section_contexts.heading: Set this up in AtlasDays
section_contexts.summary: Since the UK uses discretion rather than a strict formula, a clear travel record is still useful. In AtlasDays, you can create a custom tracker with a rolling 12-month window and keep a complete log of your UK entries and exits. That will not guarantee admission, but it does help you explain your pattern with real dates instead of guesswork.
detail_points: AtlasDays keeps a clear record of your stays so you're always prepared at the border.

articles.title: US B1/B2 Visa: Understanding the 180-Day Stay Limit
articles.headline: US B1/B2 Visa: Understanding the 180-Day Stay Limit
articles.description: How the US B1/B2 visa 180-day stay limit works, what happens if you overstay, and how to track your days in the US.
articles.intro: How the authorized stay period works, what happens if you overstay, and how to keep track of your days in the United States.

section_contexts.heading: What is the B1/B2 visa?
section_contexts.summary: The B1/B2 visa is the most common nonimmigrant visa issued by the United States. It covers two categories of short-term visitors: B1 is for business activities such as attending conferences, negotiating contracts, or consulting with business partners, while B2 is for tourism, medical treatment, and visiting family or friends. In practice, most travelers receive a combined B1/B2 visa that covers both purposes.
detail_points: The B1/B2 visa is distinct from the Visa Waiver Program (VWP) , which allows citizens of 41 countries to visit the US for up to 90 days using an ESTA authorization instead of a full visa. While the entry process is similar, the rules around length of stay and extensions differ significantly.

section_contexts.heading: The 180-day authorized stay
section_contexts.summary: A common misconception is that a B1/B2 visa automatically grants you 180 days in the United States. It does not. The visa allows you to travel to a US port of entry and request admission — the actual length of your authorized stay is determined by the Customs and Border Protection (CBP) officer at the border.
detail_points: The CBP officer reviews your travel purpose, return ticket, ties to your home country, and other factors before stamping your passport and assigning an authorized stay period. This is typically up to 180 days (six months), but the officer can grant a shorter period if they see fit. Your authorized stay date is recorded on your I-94 arrival/departure record , which is now maintained electronically.
It is critical to check your I-94 record after every entry. The date stamped in your passport and the electronic I-94 should match, but discrepancies do happen — and the I-94 is the official record.

section_contexts.heading: ESTA and the Visa Waiver Program
section_contexts.summary: If you are entering the US under the Visa Waiver Program with an approved ESTA, your maximum stay is 90 days per visit . Unlike B1/B2 visa holders, VWP travelers cannot extend their stay or change their immigration status while in the US. You also cannot appeal a denial of entry at the border.
detail_points: Short trips to Canada, Mexico, or the Caribbean generally do not reset your 90-day clock — the time spent in neighboring countries still counts toward your 90-day limit unless you return to your home country first.

Important distinction: The B1/B2 visa and the Visa Waiver Program (ESTA) are separate admission categories with different rules. B1/B2 holders can stay up to 180 days and may apply for extensions. VWP visitors are limited to 90 days with no extensions.

section_contexts.heading: The Substantial Presence Test
section_contexts.summary: Even if you stay within your authorized period, spending significant time in the US can trigger tax obligations under the Substantial Presence Test. The IRS uses a formula that counts:
detail_points: If this total reaches 183 days or more , you may be considered a US tax resident for that year and required to file a US tax return on your worldwide income. There are exemptions (such as the closer connection exception and tax treaty provisions), but the test is something every frequent US visitor should be aware of.
All days present in the current calendar year, plus
1/3 of the days present in the previous year, plus
1/6 of the days present in the year before that.

section_contexts.heading: What happens if you overstay
section_contexts.summary: Overstaying your authorized period in the US carries serious consequences:
detail_points: These bars apply from the date you leave the US, and they are difficult to waive. Prevention through accurate day-counting is far easier than dealing with the consequences.
Visa cancellation: Your existing visa is automatically voided, and you will need to apply for a new one at a US consulate before returning.
3-year bar: If you accumulate 180 days or more of unlawful presence and then depart, you are barred from re-entering the US for 3 years .
10-year bar: If you accumulate 1 year or more of unlawful presence and then depart, the re-entry bar increases to 10 years .
Future visa applications: Any overstay — even by a single day — becomes part of your immigration record and can make future visa approvals significantly harder.

section_contexts.heading: How to check your remaining days
section_contexts.summary: You can verify your authorized stay period and track your remaining days using these methods:
detail_points: For frequent travelers making multiple trips per year, manual counting across different visa categories and tax thresholds becomes increasingly error-prone.
Check your I-94 record online: Visit i94.cbp.dhs.gov to view your most recent arrival record, including your "Admit Until" date.
Review your passport stamp: The CBP officer's stamp includes your class of admission and the date you must depart by.
Count manually: If you make multiple trips, you need to track each entry and exit to ensure you are not accumulating unlawful presence or triggering the Substantial Presence Test.

section_contexts.heading: Set this up in AtlasDays
section_contexts.summary: AtlasDays lets you create a custom tracker for your US stays. You can set up a calendar-year window to monitor your Substantial Presence Test days, or a per-stay tracker to count down from your I-94 "Admit Until" date. Every entry and exit you log updates your remaining days automatically — no manual arithmetic, no missed trips.
detail_points: If you also travel to the Schengen Area or UK, each rule runs on its own tracker in the same app, so you can see all your visa windows at a glance.

This article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute immigration or legal advice. Immigration rules change frequently. Consult a qualified immigration attorney for guidance on your specific situation.

articles.title: What Counts as a 'Day' for Visa Purposes?
articles.headline: What Counts as a ‘Day’ for Visa Purposes?
articles.description: Do entry and exit days count? What about transit? How different countries count days for visa limits and residency thresholds.
articles.intro: Do entry and exit days count? What about transit? How different countries count days for visa limits and residency thresholds.

section_contexts.heading: Why it matters
section_contexts.summary: When you are tracking visa limits, a single day can be the difference between a legal stay and an overstay . Immigration rules set hard thresholds — 90 days in the Schengen Area, 183 days for tax residency in many countries — and miscounting by even one day can trigger fines, deportation, or future entry bans. The tricky part is that different countries count days differently, and the rules are rarely spelled out in plain language.

section_contexts.heading: Entry and exit days
section_contexts.summary: The most common question travellers face: do the day you arrive and the day you leave both count? In most visa regimes, yes — both the entry day and the exit day count as full days of presence . The Schengen Area is the most well-known example. If you land in Paris on March 1 and fly out on March 3, that counts as three days, not two.
detail_points: This catches people off guard because it feels intuitive to count only full 24-hour periods. But immigration authorities typically count calendar days, not hours. Even if you arrive at 11:55 PM, that entire calendar day counts.

section_contexts.heading: Transit days
section_contexts.summary: Whether a transit day counts depends on one thing: did you pass through immigration? If you cleared passport control and entered the country — even briefly, even just to catch a connecting flight from a different terminal — that day counts as a day of presence.
detail_points: Airside transit is different. If you stay within the international transit zone of an airport and never pass through border control, the transit country generally does not count that day. However, not all airports have transit zones, and some nationalities need a transit visa even for airside connections. Always check before you book.

section_contexts.heading: Partial days
section_contexts.summary: Many countries treat any part of a calendar day as a full day . It does not matter if you were in the country for 23 hours or 23 minutes — if you were present at any point during that calendar day, it counts. This is the standard approach in the Schengen Area and many other visa-free travel regimes.
detail_points: Some jurisdictions are more lenient. Certain tax residency rules, for example, may not count a day if you were in the country for fewer than a specified number of hours. But these exceptions are narrow and jurisdiction-specific. Unless you have confirmed the exact rule that applies to you, assume a partial day is a full day.

section_contexts.heading: Overnight stays vs. calendar days
section_contexts.summary: Not every system counts calendar days. Some count nights instead. The UK visitor rules historically worked this way for certain purposes — what mattered was where you spent the night, not which country you happened to be in during the daytime. This distinction can work in your favour or against you, depending on your travel pattern.
detail_points: Tax authorities sometimes use a "midnight rule": you are counted as present on a given day only if you are in the country at midnight. This means a day trip where you arrive in the morning and leave before midnight might not count. But again, this varies and you need to verify the specific rule that governs your situation.

section_contexts.heading: How different systems count
section_contexts.summary: Schengen Area: Both the entry day and the exit day count as full days. Calendar-day basis. All 29 Schengen countries share one counter.
detail_points: United States (Substantial Presence Test): For IRS tax residency, the formula weights days across three years: current-year days count fully, prior-year days count as one-third, and the year before that as one-sixth. You trigger the test at 183 weighted days — which can happen even if you spent fewer than 183 actual days in the US this year.

United Kingdom (tax residency): Under the Statutory Residence Test, the day of arrival counts but the day of departure does not. One of the few systems where entry and exit are treated asymmetrically.

CBI and Golden Visa programs: Citizenship by Investment programs often require physical presence (e.g., a minimum number of days per year). These typically count calendar days and may require proof such as flight records or utility bills.

section_contexts.heading: Where to find your specific rule
section_contexts.summary: For visa limits, check the immigration authority of the destination country — not a travel blog. For tax residency, check the revenue authority (IRS for the US, HMRC for the UK, or the equivalent in your country). Tax treaties between countries can modify how days are counted or which country gets to tax you. When significant money or residency status is at stake, an international tax advisor is worth the cost.
detail_points: The safe approach: When in doubt, count conservatively. Count both your entry and exit days. Do not plan trips that use every last day of your allowance. Give yourself a buffer of at least a few days — the cost of being cautious is far less than the cost of being wrong.

section_contexts.heading: Don't leave it to guesswork
section_contexts.summary: Day-counting rules are confusing because they seem simple until they aren't. One trip might span two visa zones with different counting methods, and a single miscounted day can cascade into a compliance problem. The safest move is to track your days with a tool that understands these rules.
detail_points: AtlasDays handles day counting so you don't have to second-guess.
Schengen 90/180-Day Rule Explained
US B1/B2 Visa: Understanding the 180-Day Stay Limit
How to Track Your Travel Days (And Why Spreadsheets Fail)
Does a Layover Count as Visiting a Country?

articles.title: When Do You Get Schengen Days Back After a 90-Day Stay?
articles.headline: When Do You Get Schengen Days Back After a 90-Day Stay?
articles.description: How Schengen days come back after a 90-day stay, why the rolling window matters, and when days free up one by one.
articles.intro: The rolling-window answer is simple: you regain days one by one as old days fall outside the 180-day lookback.

section_contexts.heading: Short answer
section_contexts.summary: You do not wait for a full 90-day reset. Under the Schengen 90/180 rule, days return one by one as each old day becomes older than 180 days and drops out of the lookback window.
detail_points: That means your allowance starts opening up gradually, not all at once.

section_contexts.heading: How the rolling window works
section_contexts.summary: Think of the rule as a sliding 180-day frame. On any date, you look back 180 days and count how many of those days you spent in the Schengen Area. If the total is 90, you are out of days. When the oldest day in that window drops out, you regain one day.
detail_points: This is why the answer to “when do days come back?” depends on the date of your earliest Schengen day, not just on the date you left.

section_contexts.heading: Worked example
section_contexts.summary: Say you spent 90 consecutive days in Schengen from 1 January to 31 March .
detail_points: The pattern continues the same way after that. As each older travel day leaves the 180-day window, one more day becomes available.
Key idea: the window does not “reset.” It keeps moving. If you want to know whether you can re-enter today, the real question is how many Schengen days are still inside the last 180 days.

section_contexts.heading: What this means for planning
section_contexts.summary: If you used your full allowance in one long trip, you usually regain days gradually over the next 90 days or so, depending on when those days fall out of the lookback.
detail_points: If you took several shorter trips, the freed-up days may appear in a less obvious pattern because the oldest trip days fall out first.
If you are planning a return trip, count backward from the date you want to enter, not forward from the day you left.

section_contexts.heading: Where people get tripped up
section_contexts.summary: The common mistake is thinking “90 days out means I get 90 fresh days.” That is not how the Schengen rule works. The correct approach is to check the last 180 days on the day you want to travel and count the days still inside that period.
detail_points: That is also why a trip tracker is useful. A spreadsheet can tell you what happened before, but it is easy to lose track of how one new trip affects every future date.
If you want the full rule explained from the top, read the main Schengen guide and the rolling-window walkthrough next.
Schengen 90/180-Day Rule Explained
How the Schengen Rolling Window Actually Works
What Counts as a "Day" for Visa Purposes?

images.prompt:
Create a 3:2 website illustration for atlasdays.app.
This image supports an evergreen educational travel-rule article. It should feel authoritative, clear, and SEO-friendly, with one dominant idea that still reads well on a responsive web page while still feeling like part of a premium travel brand.
Use a premium travel-tech editorial illustration style that fits AtlasDays: dark charcoal and cool slate as the base, deep oceanic navy depth, cyan-blue route accents, pale ice-blue surfaces, and small restrained warm highlights for contrast. The image should feel premium, modern, mobile-first, and travel-oriented: calm but not sterile, precise but not academic, aspirational without becoming generic travel-advertising fluff. Favor layered depth, subtle glow, motion, route energy, and destination atmosphere over flat icon-pack graphics. Do not make it whimsical, photoreal stock-photo-like, or cartoonish.
Topic concept: a recognizable Europe map, a rolling travel window, and precise travel-day counting.
Supporting context: How Schengen days come back after a 90-day stay, why the rolling window matters, and when days free up one by one.
This artwork will appear beneath an existing webpage title and subtitle, so the image itself must not contain any heading, subtitle, sentence, or label. Minimal threshold numerals are acceptable, but avoid poster-style copy or UI-like text.
Do not make the image look like a poster, slide, infographic header, or book cover.
Use a dark neutral background that separates cleanly from both the light and dark website themes. Blue should appear as an accent, not as a full flat background.
Visual focus: a recognizable Europe map, a rolling travel window, and precise travel-day counting.
Helpful motifs: Europe outline, calendar window, route arcs, day-count markers.
The image should feel like it belongs to a product people are excited to use for travel, not just a neutral reference article.
Bring in some sense of journey, movement, place, or destination atmosphere while staying clean and legible on the page.
When the image uses paper, cards, tables, or document-like surfaces, tint them with pale ice-blue, mist blue, or cool blue-gray rather than stark pure white so they integrate better with light mode.
Avoid large flat white blocks. Keep highlights soft, slightly cool, and premium.
Keep the image subtly connected to AtlasDays by using travel-tracking-adjacent motifs such as route lines, day-count chips, tidy cards, world highlights, or private-device context, but do not show a literal app screenshot.
Avoid readable text, letters, words, labels, titles, map labels, typographic elements, UI screenshots, government seals, watermarks, stock-photo cliches, passport-stamp collages, fake official forms, brand logos, or cluttered layouts. Avoid stiff textbook-diagram energy, generic corporate SaaS art, or dry flat iconography. If a counting concept is needed, represent it abstractly with bars, chips, rings, windows, or markers. Minimal numerals are acceptable only when they function as an abstract threshold symbol rather than readable UI text or copy.
Intro context: "The rolling-window answer is simple: you regain days one by one as old days fall outside the 180-day lookback."
Additional global art direction: Keep the images calm, premium, and clear on a documentation-heavy website. Favor strong silhouettes, clean geometry, and a travel-compliance tone over busy scenes or stock-photo energy.

images.alt: Supporting illustration for When Do You Get Schengen Days Back After a 90-Day Stay?, focused on where people get tripped up in the AtlasDays visual style.

images.theme_focus: a recognizable Europe map, a rolling travel window, and precise travel-day counting
images.context_summary: The common mistake is thinking “90 days out means I get 90 fresh days.” That is not how the Schengen rule works. The correct approach is to check the last 180 days on the day you want to travel and count the days still inside that period.
